## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** *Define the Autoregressive (AR) model used in EEG signal modelling. State the conditions under which it is considered a valid parametric representation of the EEG. (3 marks)*

**[Model Answer]**

The AR model of order $p$ represents the current EEG sample $x(n)$ as a weighted linear combination of its $p$ past samples plus a white noise innovation $w(n)$:

$$x(n) = -\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k x(n-k) + w(n)$$

where $a_k$ are the AR coefficients and $w(n) \sim N(0, \sigma^2)$ is zero-mean white Gaussian noise. The model is valid when:

1. The EEG signal is reasonably stationary within the analysis epoch.
2. The model order $p$ is chosen using AIC or MDL (typically 6 to 12 for EEG).
3. The driving noise $w(n)$ is zero-mean white Gaussian.
4. The poles of the corresponding filter $H(z) = 1/[1 + \sum a_k z^{-k}]$ lie **inside the unit circle** (stability condition $\vert z_i \vert < 1$), ensuring a stationary random process.

**Tag:** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

---

**Q2.** *Distinguish between physiological and non-physiological artefacts in EEG recording. Give two examples of each. (3 marks)*

**[Model Answer]**

| Aspect | Physiological Artefacts | Non-Physiological Artefacts |
|--------|-------------------------|------------------------------|
| Origin | Originate from the subject's body (non-cerebral) | Originate from equipment, environment, or recording setup |
| Example 1 | **Ocular (EOG)** — Eye blinks and saccades | **Power-line interference** at **50 Hz / 60 Hz** |
| Example 2 | **Muscular (EMG)** — Neck and facial muscle activity | **Electrode pop** due to impedance changes |

Physiological artefacts are inherent to the subject and require biological reference signals (EOG, EMG) for regression or ICA-based removal. Non-physiological artefacts are deterministic in nature and can typically be suppressed by proper shielding, notch filtering, or improved electrode contact.

**Tag:** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

**QUESTION A — Option 1 (14 Marks)**

**(a)** *Derive the Yule-Walker equations for an AR(p) model. Using these equations, obtain the linear predictor coefficients and the white noise variance $\sigma^2$. (7 marks)*

**(b)** *Explain the Time-Varying AR (TVAR) model for non-stationary EEG signals. Derive the LMS update rule for the TVAR coefficients and discuss the choice of step-size $\mu$. (7 marks)*

---

**(a) Model Solution — Yule-Walker Derivation (7 marks)**

**Step 1** — AR model definition: Starting from $x(n) = -\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k x(n-k) + w(n)$ ... (1 mark)

**Step 2** — Multiply by $x(n-k)$ and take expectation. Using $r_x(k) = \mathbb{E}\{x(n) x(n-k)\}$ and noting $w(n)$ is uncorrelated with past samples: $r_x(k) + \sum_{i=1}^{p} a_i r_x(k-i) = 0$ for $k \geq 1$ ... (2 marks)

**Step 3** — Write the $p$ simultaneous equations as $\mathbf{R}\mathbf{a} = -\mathbf{r}$, where $\mathbf{R}$ is the $p \times p$ Toeplitz autocorrelation matrix. Solution by Levinson-Durbin gives the coefficients $a_1, \ldots, a_p$ in $O(p^2)$ operations ... (2 marks)

**Step 4** — White noise variance: $\sigma^2 = r_x(0) + \sum_{i=1}^{p} a_i r_x(i)$ ... (1 mark)

**Step 5** — Stability: All poles of $1 + \sum a_k z^{-k} = 0$ must satisfy $\vert z \vert < 1$ ... (1 mark)

---

**(b) Model Solution — TVAR with LMS (7 marks)**

**Step 1** — Non-stationarity: EEG exhibits amplitude and frequency modulations (sleep spindles, alpha bursts, seizure evolution). Stationary AR fails. Introduce $a_k(n)$ ... (1 mark)

**Step 2** — TVAR model: $x(n) = -\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k(n) x(n-k) + w(n)$ ... (1 mark)

**Step 3** — Define regressor $\mathbf{x}(n) = [x(n-1), \ldots, x(n-p)]^T$ and coefficient vector $\mathbf{a}(n)$. Predict: $\hat{x}(n) = -\mathbf{a}^T(n-1) \mathbf{x}(n)$ ... (1 mark)

**Step 4** — Error: $e(n) = x(n) - \hat{x}(n)$. Cost: $J = \frac{1}{2} e^2(n)$ ... (1 mark)

**Step 5** — Gradient descent: $\mathbf{a}(n) = \mathbf{a}(n-1) + \mu e(n) \mathbf{x}(n)$ (or with factor 2 by convention) ... (1 mark)

**Step 6** — Stability and step-size choice:
- Bound: $0 < \mu < 1/(2 \lambda_{\max}) \approx 1/(2 p \, r_x(0))$
- Trade-off: Large $\mu$ → fast tracking, high misadjustment; Small $\mu$ → slow tracking, low misadjustment.
- Practical EEG range: $10^{-5} \leq \mu \leq 10^{-2}$ depending on sampling rate and signal power.
- Alternative: RLS with forgetting factor $\lambda \in [0.98, 1]$ for faster convergence ... (2 marks)

**Tag:** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024 / Set A]`

---

**QUESTION B — Option 2 (14 Marks)**

**(a)** *Explain the AR(p) parametric model for EEG signal representation. State and derive the AR-based power spectral density formula. (7 marks)*

**(b)** *Classify the various types of artefacts encountered in EEG recording. Discuss the methods used to remove ocular (EOG) and muscle (EMG) artefacts. (7 marks)*

---

**(a) Model Solution — AR Model and AR-PSD (7 marks)**

**Step 1** — AR(p) model: $x(n) = -\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k x(n-k) + w(n)$ where $w(n)$ is white Gaussian noise with variance $\sigma^2$ ... (1 mark)

**Step 2** — Z-domain transfer function: $H(z) = 1/[1 + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k z^{-k}]$. AR model = all-pole filter ... (1 mark)

**Step 3** — Output PSD: For an LTI system driven by white noise, $P_{xx}(z) = \sigma^2 H(z) H(z^{-1})$ ... (1 mark)

**Step 4** — On the unit circle $z = e^{j2\pi f T}$:

$$P_{xx}(f) = \frac{\sigma^2}{\left\vert 1 + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k e^{-j2\pi f k T} \right\vert^2}$$ ... (2 marks)

**Step 5** — Advantages: Higher spectral resolution than FFT, suitable for short data segments, parametric peaks match dominant EEG rhythms ... (1 mark)

**Step 6** — Disadvantages: Model order selection required, less robust to non-stationarity (use TVAR instead) ... (1 mark)

---

**(b) Model Solution — Artefact Classification and Removal (7 marks)**

**Step 1** — Classification of EEG artefacts (1 mark):

**(i) Physiological artefacts:**
- **Ocular (EOG):** Eye blinks (delta-like 0–4 Hz deflection up to several hundred µV), eye movements (slow horizontal/vertical deflections)
- **Muscular (EMG):** High-frequency activity (20–300 Hz) from scalp, neck, and facial muscles
- **Cardiac (ECG/EKG):** Pulse-related QRS bursts appearing in some channels
- **Sweat and slow skin potentials:** 0.25–0.5 Hz drift

**(ii) Non-physiological / Technical artefacts:**
- **Power-line interference:** Sinusoidal contamination at 50 Hz (India) or 60 Hz (US)
- **Electrode pop:** Sharp transients from electrode-skin impedance changes
- **Cable movement / Lead artefact:** Motion-induced capacitive pickups
- **Instrumental noise:** Amplifier drift, ADC quantisation

**Step 2** — Ocular artefact removal methods (3 marks):
- **EOG regression / subtraction:** Record bipolar EOG (vertical and horizontal). Regress each EEG channel against EOG: $x_{\text{clean}} = x_{\text{EEG}} - \beta \cdot x_{\text{EOG}}$. Calibration of regression coefficients $\beta$ is required.
- **Adaptive filtering (LMS):** Use EOG as reference input to an adaptive noise canceller (ANC) with EEG as primary.
- **Independent Component Analysis (ICA):** Decompose multichannel EEG into independent sources. Ocular sources (high kurtosis, frontal topography) are identified and removed by zeroing the corresponding rows in the unmixing matrix.

**Step 3** — EMG artefact removal methods (3 marks):
- **Low-pass filtering:** Cut-off at 30–40 Hz removes most muscle activity (but loses gamma-band neural content).
- **Wavelet thresholding:** Decompose EEG using DWT (e.g., Daubechies-4). Threshold the detail coefficients at scales corresponding to EMG (high-frequency sub-bands). Soft thresholding: $c_{\text{new}} = \text{sign}(c) \max(0, \vert c \vert - T)$.
- **Canonical Correlation Analysis (CCA):** Exploits autocorrelation differences — neural signals are autocorrelated, EMG is broadband. Top CCA components are muscle artefacts.
- **ICA + automated classification:** Automated methods (e.g., SASICA, ICLabel) classify ICA components and reject EMG-dominant ones.

**Tag:** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / Set B]`

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the negative sign convention** in the AR model. KTU expects $x(n) = -\sum a_k x(n-k) + w(n)$ — omitting the negative sign leads to sign-flipped coefficients and unstable models.
> 2. **Stating the LMS update with the wrong factor of 2.** Be explicit: if $J = \frac{1}{2} e^2$, the update becomes $a_k(n) = a_k(n-1) + 2\mu e(n) x(n-k)$; if $J = e^2$, it is $a_k(n) = a_k(n-1) + \mu e(n) x(n-k)$. State your convention.
> 3. **Confusing the stability bound.** The bound is $0 < \mu < 1 / \lambda_{\max}$, not $0 < \mu < 1$. Using $\lambda_{\max}$ is critical.
> 4. **Mixing up EOG and EMG properties.** EOG = low-frequency, high-amplitude, frontal. EMG = high-frequency, low-amplitude, diffuse. Examiners specifically test this distinction.
> 5. **Skipping the AIC/MDL model order selection discussion.** KTU 2024 specifically requires mention of model order determination in the AR section.
> 6. **Forgetting the unit circle stability condition** $\vert z_i \vert < 1$ for AR poles. Always mention this when discussing model validity.
> 7. **Omitting units** in derivations — EEG amplitudes are in **µV**, frequency in **Hz**, time in seconds, sampling rate $F_s$ in **Hz**.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 3**
>
> - **EEG Fundamentals**: amplitude **10–200 µV**, bandwidth **0.5–100 Hz**, clinical band **0.5–30 Hz**; five canonical rhythms δ, θ, α, β, γ.
> - **AR(p) Model**: $x(n) = -\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k x(n-k) + w(n)$; parameters $\{a_k, \sigma^2\}$ estimated by Yule-Walker, Burg, or LS.
> - **AR-PSD**: $P_{xx}(f) = \sigma^2 / \left\vert 1 + \sum a_k e^{-j2\pi f k} \right\vert^2$ — gives sharp spectral peaks at dominant EEG rhythms.
> - **Yule-Walker**: Toeplitz system $\mathbf{R}\mathbf{a} = -\mathbf{r}$; solved efficiently by Levinson-Durbin in $O(p^2)$.
> - **Model Order**: $p \approx 6$–$12$ for EEG, chosen by AIC = $N \ln(\sigma_p^2) + 2p$ or MDL.
> - **Stability**: All AR poles must lie inside the unit circle $\vert z \vert < 1$.
> - **TVAR Model**: $x(n) = -\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k(n) x(n-k) + w(n)$ — tracks non-stationarities of EEG.
> - **LMS Update**: $a_k(n) = a_k(n-1) + 2\mu e(n) x(n-k)$, $0 < \mu < 1/\lambda_{\max}$, trade-off between tracking speed and misadjustment.
> - **RLS Update**: $\mathbf{a}(n) = \mathbf{a}(n-1) - \mathbf{k}(n) e(n)$ with forgetting factor $\lambda \in [0.98, 1]$; faster convergence, $O(p^2)$ cost.
> - **Artefacts Classification**:
>   - *Physiological*: EOG (low-freq, high-amp), EMG (high-freq, low-amp), ECG (QRS bursts), sweat (slow drift), movement.
>   - *Non-physiological*: power line (50/60 Hz), electrode pop, cable motion, instrument noise.
> - **Artefact Removal Methods**: Notch filter (50/60 Hz), EOG regression, adaptive LMS/ANC, ICA, wavelet thresholding, CCA.
> - **Processing Pipeline**: Notch → Bandpass (0.5–70 Hz) → ICA → Wavelet → TVAR modelling → Spectral features → Classification.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Artefact Reduction Methods - Linear Filtering
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT755 - ADVANCED BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Artefact Reduction Methods - Linear Filtering

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ARTEFACT REDUCTION METHODS – LINEAR FILTERING

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Artefact (Contamination):** In the context of biomedical signal processing, an *artefact* (or *artifact*) is defined as any unwanted component superimposed on a physiological signal of interest, originating from sources **other than the biological system under investigation**. These contaminations obscure the diagnostic information carried by the true bioelectric, biomechanical, or biochemical signal.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Linear Filtering for Artefact Reduction:** Linear filtering is a signal processing technique in which the input signal $x[n]$ is convolved with the impulse response $h[n]$ of a Linear Time-Invariant (LTI) system to produce an output $y[n]$ that retains the desired physiological component while suppressing the unwanted artefact component, exploiting the principle of **superposition** and **frequency selectivity**.

Mathematically, the linear filtering operation is expressed as:

$$y[n] = x[n] * h[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{+\infty} x[k] \cdot h[n-k]$$

The corresponding frequency-domain representation is given by:

$$Y(e^{j\omega}) = X(e^{j\omega}) \cdot H(e^{j\omega})$$

where $H(e^{j\omega})$ is the **Discrete-Time Fourier Transform (DTFT)** of the filter impulse response and acts as a multiplicative gain function that selectively attenuates artefact-bearing frequency bands.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **🎧 The Headphone Equalizer Analogy**
> Imagine you are listening to a music track on headphones, but the recording has an annoying low-frequency hum from a nearby generator. To eliminate the hum, you press the **"Bass Cut"** button on your equalizer — this is precisely what a **high-pass filter** does in biomedical signal processing.
>
> In an ECG recording contaminated by **50 Hz powerline interference**, a **notch filter** acts like a **surgical scalpel** that removes a razor-thin slice of the frequency spectrum centred exactly at 50 Hz, leaving the rest of the signal completely untouched.
>
> Think of a linear filter as a **bouncer at a club door**: it checks the "frequency ID" of every component in the signal and only allows the desired frequency guests (the biological signal) to enter the output, politely rejecting the unwanted ones (the artefacts).

---

## 1.3 Classification of Common Biomedical Artefacts

| Artefact Type | Source | Frequency Band | Recommended Linear Filter |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Powerline Interference** | 50/60 Hz mains supply, electrodes | Narrow band at **50 Hz / 60 Hz** | Notch (Band-stop) Filter |
| **Baseline Wander** | Respiration, electrode movement | **0 – 0.5 Hz** | High-pass Filter |
| **High-Frequency Noise** | EMG, instrumentation noise | **> 40 Hz (ECG)** | Low-pass Filter |
| **Motion Artefact** | Patient movement, skin stretch | **0.1 – 10 Hz** | Adaptive / Wiener Filter |
| **Thermal (Johnson) Noise** | Electronic components | **Broadband (white)** | Low-pass / Smoothing Filter |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **standard metrics** universally accepted in KTU 2024 evaluation for filter performance are:
> - **Cut-off frequency** ($f_c$) in **Hz**
> - **Order of filter** ($N$) — dimensionless
> - **Passband ripple** ($\delta_p$) in **dB**
> - **Stopband attenuation** ($\delta_s$) in **dB**
> - **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)** improvement in **dB**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation of Linear Filtering for Artefact Removal

The theoretical underpinning of linear filtering for artefact reduction rests on **three core assumptions**:

1. **Signal-Stationarity in Short Windows:** The physiological signal is assumed to be *quasi-stationary* over the analysis window, allowing frequency-domain decomposition.
2. **Frequency Separability:** The artefact occupies a *distinguishable* spectral region from the signal of interest.
3. **Linearity & Time-Invariance:** The filter satisfies $H\{a \cdot x_1[n] + b \cdot x_2[n]\} = a \cdot H\{x_1[n]\} + b \cdot H\{x_2[n]\}$ and is invariant to time shifts.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Linear Filtering?**
> Linear filters are preferred in KTU-aligned biomedical applications because they introduce **no new frequency components** (no harmonic distortion), preserve signal **phase relationships** when designed as zero-phase filters, and possess **mathematically tractable** closed-form transfer functions.

---

## 2.2 The Four Principal Linear Filter Architectures

### 2.2.1 Low-Pass Filter (LPF) — Anti-aliasing & High-Frequency Noise Suppression
Removes noise components whose frequency **exceeds** the signal's useful bandwidth. Used to suppress EMG contamination in ECG and thermal noise in EEG.

### 2.2.2 High-Pass Filter (HPF) — Baseline Wander Removal
Eliminates very low-frequency drifts caused by respiration and electrode-skin interface changes. Standard cut-off for ECG baseline removal: **0.5 Hz**.

### 2.2.3 Band-Pass Filter (BPF) — Band-of-Interest Isolation
Cascaded LPF + HPF to extract a specific physiological band, e.g., **0.5 – 40 Hz** for clinical ECG analysis.

### 2.2.4 Band-Stop (Notch) Filter — Powerline Interference Elimination
A narrow rejection filter centred at the powerline frequency. For Indian/Kerala grid: **50 Hz notch**; for US-standard: **60 Hz notch**.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Examination-Ready)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations. They are the "weapons" for the KTU 14-mark derivations.**

| # | Formula / Equation | Description | Standard Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $y[n] = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} h[k] \cdot x[n-k]$ | Convolution sum (FIR filter) | Dimensionless |
| 2 | $H(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} h[k] \cdot e^{-j\omega k}$ | Frequency response of FIR filter | Dimensionless magnitude |
| 3 | $H(s) = \frac{b_0 + b_1 s + \dots + b_M s^M}{a_0 + a_1 s + \dots + a_N s^N}$ | General IIR transfer function (Laplace) | Dimensionless |
| 4 | $f_c = \frac{\omega_c}{2\pi} = \frac{1}{2\pi R C}$ | RC-LPF cut-off frequency | **Hz** |
| 5 | $f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi R C}$ | RC-HPF cut-off frequency | **Hz** |
| 6 | $H(z) = \frac{1 - 2\cos(\omega_0)z^{-1} + z^{-2}}{1 - 2r\cos(\omega_0)z^{-1} + r^2 z^{-2}}$ | 2nd-order IIR Notch filter (DFT-II form) | Dimensionless |
| 7 | $\omega_0 = \frac{2\pi f_0}{f_s}$ | Normalized digital notch frequency | **radians/sample** |
| 8 | $\Delta f = \frac{f_s}{N}$ | Frequency resolution (DFT bins) | **Hz** |
| 9 | $r = 1 - \frac{\pi \cdot BW}{f_s}$ | Pole radius (controls notch bandwidth $BW$) | Dimensionless, $0 < r < 1$ |
| 10 | $H(z) = \frac{1 - z^{-N}}{N}$ | Moving Average (MA) LPF transfer function | Dimensionless |
| 11 | $\vert H(e^{j\omega}) \vert = \frac{1}{N} \left\vert \frac{\sin(\omega N / 2)}{\sin(\omega / 2)} \right\vert$ | Magnitude response of MA filter (Dirichlet kernel) | Dimensionless |
| 12 | $H_{filt}(z) = \frac{2 - 2\cos(\omega_0)}{2 - 2r\cos(\omega_0)} \cdot \frac{1 - 2\cos(\omega_0)z^{-1} + z^{-2}}{1 - 2r\cos(\omega_0)z^{-1} + r^2 z^{-2}}$ | **Normalized** IIR Notch (unity gain at DC & Nyquist) | Dimensionless |
| 13 | $H_{HPF}(z) = 1 - H_{LPF}(z)$ | Complementary filter relationship | Dimensionless |
| 14 | $\text{SNR}_{out} = 10 \log_{10} \left( \frac{\sigma_s^2}{\sigma_n^2} \right) \text{ dB}$ | Output Signal-to-Noise Ratio | **dB** |
| 15 | $\text{SNR}_{improvement} = \text{SNR}_{out} - \text{SNR}_{in}$ | Filter performance metric | **dB** |

> [!NOTE]
> In all KTU board examinations, examiners **specifically reward** the writing of design equations (1, 4, 5, 6, 7, 9, 12) with the **first 2–3 marks** of any design problem. Do not skip them.

---

## 2.4 Engineering Utility and Real-World Applications

- **Clinical ECG Machines (Philips, GE, Schiller):** Use 0.5–40 Hz band-pass + 50 Hz notch as the standard pre-processing chain.
- **ICU Patient Monitors:** Real-time cascaded LPF + HPF for arrhythmia detection.
- **Polysomnography (Sleep Lab EEG):** 0.5–30 Hz band-pass with 50 Hz notch for sleep staging.
- **EMG-based Prosthetics:** 20–500 Hz band-pass for muscle signal extraction.
- **Implantable Pacemakers:** On-chip IIR filters due to strict power constraints.
- **Wearable Fitness Devices (smartwatches):** Adaptive linear filters running on low-power MCUs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **trade-off** every biomedical engineer must confront: **Sharper filter roll-off ↔ Higher filter order ↔ Greater group delay** (signal distortion in time domain). The KTU 2024 examiner expects you to discuss this **explicitly** in design problems.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

> [!NOTE]
> Below are **three full derivations** that are repeatedly asked in KTU University Examinations. Master each one.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1: 2nd-Order IIR Notch Filter at 50 Hz (Powerline Artefact)

### Problem Setup
- Sampling frequency: $f_s = 500$ Hz (standard for ECG per AAMI/ANSI standards)
- Notch frequency: $f_0 = 50$ Hz
- Desired notch bandwidth: $BW = 4$ Hz (to remove only the interference and not ECG components)
- Filter type: 2nd-order IIR (infinite impulse response, computationally efficient)

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Convert the notch frequency to normalized digital angular frequency:**

$$\omega_0 = \frac{2\pi f_0}{f_s} = \frac{2 \pi \times 50}{500} = \frac{\pi}{5} \text{ radians/sample}$$

This dimensionless quantity tells us where in the digital spectrum (0 to $\pi$) the notch will be placed.

**Step 2 — Compute the pole-radius $r$ that controls the notch bandwidth:**

$$r = 1 - \frac{\pi \cdot BW}{f_s} = 1 - \frac{\pi \times 4}{500} = 1 - 0.02513 = 0.97487$$

A value of $r$ very close to 1 produces a **narrow, sharp notch**, which is what we want for surgical 50 Hz removal.

**Step 3 — Compute $\cos(\omega_0)$ for the filter coefficients:**

$$\cos(\omega_0) = \cos\left(\frac{\pi}{5}\right) = \cos(36^\circ) = 0.80902$$

**Step 4 — Construct the denominator (pole) polynomial coefficients:**

$$a_1 = -2r\cos(\omega_0) = -2 \times 0.97487 \times 0.80902 = -1.5773$$

$$a_2 = r^2 = (0.97487)^2 = 0.95037$$

**Step 5 — Construct the numerator (zero) polynomial coefficients:**

$$b_0 = 1, \quad b_1 = -2\cos(\omega_0) = -1.61803, \quad b_2 = 1$$

These zeros are placed on the unit circle at angles $\pm \omega_0$, forcing the magnitude response to **zero** exactly at 50 Hz.

**Step 6 — Normalize the filter for unity gain at DC ($\omega = 0$) and at Nyquist ($\omega = \pi$):**

$$\text{Gain}_{DC} = \frac{1 - 2\cos(\omega_0) + 1}{1 - 2r\cos(\omega_0) + r^2} = \frac{2 - 2(0.80902)}{1 - 2(0.97487)(0.80902) + (0.97487)^2}$$

$$\text{Gain}_{DC} = \frac{0.38197}{0.00063} = 606.3$$

To normalize, we multiply the numerator coefficients by the factor:

$$K = \frac{1 - 2r\cos(\omega_0) + r^2}{2 - 2\cos(\omega_0)} = \frac{0.00063}{0.38197} = 0.00165$$

**Step 7 — Final Normalized Transfer Function:**

$$H(z) = 0.00165 \cdot \frac{1 - 1.61803 \, z^{-1} + z^{-2}}{1 - 1.5773 \, z^{-1} + 0.95037 \, z^{-2}}$$

This is the **complete, exam-ready** IIR notch filter for 50 Hz powerline removal.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the KTU answer sheet, after writing the transfer function, **always state** the difference equation (which is what the digital processor actually executes):
> $$y[n] = 0.00165 \cdot x[n] - 0.00267 \cdot x[n-1] + 0.00165 \cdot x[n-2] + 1.5773 \cdot y[n-1] - 0.95037 \cdot y[n-2]$$
> This step alone is worth **2 marks** in KTU valuation.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Moving Average Filter — High-Frequency Noise Smoothing

### Problem Setup
- A 5-point moving average filter ($N = 5$) is applied to a noisy ECG signal sampled at $f_s = 360$ Hz.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Write the impulse response of the MA filter:**

$$h[k] = \frac{1}{N} \quad \text{for} \quad k = 0, 1, 2, 3, 4$$

So $h[0] = h[1] = h[2] = h[3] = h[4] = 0.2$.

**Step 2 — Derive the Z-domain transfer function:**

$$H(z) = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} h[k] \cdot z^{-k} = \frac{1}{5} \sum_{k=0}^{4} z^{-k} = \frac{1}{5}(1 + z^{-1} + z^{-2} + z^{-3} + z^{-4})$$

Using the geometric series formula:

$$H(z) = \frac{1}{5} \cdot \frac{1 - z^{-5}}{1 - z^{-1}}$$

**Step 3 — Derive the magnitude response by substituting $z = e^{j\omega}$:**

$$H(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{1}{5} \cdot \frac{1 - e^{-j5\omega}}{1 - e^{-j\omega}}$$

Using Euler's identity and factoring:

$$H(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{1}{5} \cdot e^{-j2\omega} \cdot \frac{\sin(5\omega/2)}{\sin(\omega/2)}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the magnitude (dropping the linear-phase term $e^{-j2\omega}$):**

$$\vert H(e^{j\omega}) \vert = \frac{1}{5} \left\vert \frac{\sin(5\omega/2)}{\sin(\omega/2)} \right\vert$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate at $\omega = 0$ (DC gain) — should be unity:**

$$\vert H(e^{j0}) \vert = \frac{1}{5} \cdot \lim_{\omega \to 0} \frac{\sin(5\omega/2)}{\sin(\omega/2)} = \frac{1}{5} \cdot \frac{5}{1} = 1 \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 6 — Identify the first null (cut-off behavior) — occurs at $\omega = 2\pi/5$:**

$$\sin(5\omega/2) = 0 \implies \frac{5\omega}{2} = \pi \implies \omega_{null} = \frac{2\pi}{5}$$

In Hz: $f_{null} = \frac{\omega_{null} \cdot f_s}{2\pi} = \frac{(2\pi/5) \times 360}{2\pi} = 72$ Hz

> [!NOTE]
> This means the moving average filter's first null is at 72 Hz, providing approximately **24 dB attenuation** at 72 Hz — more than enough to suppress high-frequency EMG noise contaminating the ECG.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Complementary High-Pass Filter for Baseline Wander Removal

### Problem Setup
- ECG signal sampled at $f_s = 500$ Hz.
- A first-order IIR Butterworth high-pass filter with $f_c = 0.5$ Hz is required to remove baseline wander.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Compute the pre-warped analog cut-off frequency (to prevent warping distortion in bilinear transform):**

$$\Omega_c = 2 f_s \tan\left(\frac{\pi f_c}{f_s}\right) = 2 \times 500 \times \tan\left(\frac{\pi \times 0.5}{500}\right)$$

$$\Omega_c = 1000 \times \tan(0.003142) = 1000 \times 0.003142 = 3.1416 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 2 — Design the analog prototype first-order HPF:**

$$H_a(s) = \frac{s}{s + \Omega_c} = \frac{s}{s + 3.1416}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the bilinear transform $s = 2 f_s \cdot \frac{1 - z^{-1}}{1 + z^{-1}}$:**

$$s = 2 \times 500 \times \frac{1 - z^{-1}}{1 + z^{-1}} = 1000 \cdot \frac{1 - z^{-1}}{1 + z^{-1}}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute and simplify:**

$$H(z) = \frac{1000 \cdot \frac{1 - z^{-1}}{1 + z^{-1}}}{1000 \cdot \frac{1 - z^{-1}}{1 + z^{-1}} + 3.1416}$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator by $(1 + z^{-1})$:

$$H(z) = \frac{1000 (1 - z^{-1})}{1000(1 - z^{-1}) + 3.1416(1 + z^{-1})}$$

$$H(z) = \frac{1000(1 - z^{-1})}{(1003.1416) + (996.8584) z^{-1}}$$

**Step 5 — Normalize the denominator coefficient to 1 (standard form):**

$$H(z) = \frac{0.99687(1 - z^{-1})}{1 + 0.99373 z^{-1}}$$

**Step 6 — Difference equation (executable code form):**

$$y[n] = 0.99687 \cdot x[n] - 0.99687 \cdot x[n-1] - 0.99373 \cdot y[n-1]$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verification check** at $\omega = 0$ (DC, which baseline wander occupies):
> $$H(e^{j0}) = \frac{0.99687(1 - 1)}{1 + 0.99373} = 0 \quad \checkmark$$
> DC is **completely blocked**, confirming correct HPF design.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Filter Selection Decision Table

| Signal / Artefact | Frequency Separation | Best Linear Filter | Filter Order | Typical Cut-off |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| ECG (clean) | 0.5 – 40 Hz | Band-pass | 2nd order Butterworth | HPF: 0.5 Hz, LPF: 40 Hz |
| 50 Hz hum in ECG | 50 Hz narrow | IIR Notch | 2nd order IIR | $f_0 = 50$ Hz, $BW = 4$ Hz |
| Baseline drift in EEG | < 0.1 Hz | High-pass | 1st order Butterworth | 0.1 Hz |
| EMG in ECG | > 40 Hz | Low-pass | 4th order Butterworth | 40 Hz |
| Motion artefact in PPG | 0.1 – 10 Hz | Moving Average | $N = 8$ to $N = 16$ | Adaptive $N$ |
| Respiratory in ECG | 0.1 – 0.5 Hz | High-pass | 2nd order Bessel | 0.5 Hz |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

> [!NOTE]
> All diagrams below use Mermaid syntax with strict alphanumeric node IDs (no reserved keywords used as bare names).

---

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Complete Artefact Reduction Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[rawBiopotentialSignal] --> B[PreAmplifierStage]
    B --> C[AntiAliasingLPF]
    C --> D[AnalogToDigitalConverter]
    D --> E[DigitalNotchFilter50Hz]
    E --> F[DigitalHighPassFilter0_5Hz]
    F --> G[DigitalLowPassFilter40Hz]
    G --> H[CleanProcessedSignal]
    G --> I[FeatureExtractionQRS]
    I --> J[DiagnosticDecision]
```

---

## 4.2 Classification Tree of Linear Filters for Artefact Reduction

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[LinearFiltersForArtefactReduction] --> B[FrequencySelective]
    A --> C[StatisticalOptimal]
    A --> D[Adaptive]
    B --> B1[LowPassLPF]
    B --> B2[HighPassHPF]
    B --> B3[BandPassBPF]
    B --> B4[BandStopNotch]
    C --> C1[WienerFilter]
    C --> C2[MatchedFilter]
    D --> D1[LMSAlgorithm]
    D --> D2[RLSAlgorithm]
    B1 --> B1A[Butterworth]
    B1 --> B1B[Chebyshev]
    B1 --> B1C[MovingAverage]
    B2 --> B2A[Butterworth]
    B2 --> B2B[Bessel]
    B4 --> B4A[IIRNotch]
    B4 --> B4B[FIRNotch]
```

---

## 4.3 Signal Flow: Notch Filter Internal Architecture (Direct Form II)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    X[x_n_input] --> S1((SUM1))
    S1 --> Z1[Z^-1 Delay]
    Z1 --> Z2[Z^-1 Delay]
    Z1 --> S1
    Z2 --> S1
    S1 --> S2((SUM2))
    S2 --> Y[y_n_output]
    S2 --> W1[Weight_b1]
    W1 --> S1
    S2 --> W2[Weight_b2]
    W2 --> Z1
    S2 --> W3[Weight_a1]
    W3 --> S2
    S2 --> W4[Weight_a2]
    W4 --> Z2
```

---

## 4.4 Functional Architecture: Adaptive Linear Filter (LMS-based Motion Artefact Cancellation)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[primarySignalECGplusMotion] --> B[AdaptiveFilterFIR]
    A --> C[AdaptiveFilterFIR]
    C --> E[ErrorComputation]
    R[referenceSignalAccelerometer] --> B
    B --> D[outputYhatEstimatedArtefact]
    D --> E
    E --> F[LMSWeightUpdateEquation]
    F --> B
    E --> G[cleanECG_output]
    A --> G
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the LMS adaptive filter, the **weight update equation** is the heart of the system:
> $$\mathbf{w}[n+1] = \mathbf{w}[n] + 2\mu \cdot e[n] \cdot \mathbf{x}[n]$$
> where $\mu$ is the step-size (convergence parameter), $e[n]$ is the error between the actual contaminated input and the estimated artefact, and $\mathbf{x}[n]$ is the reference accelerometer signal vector.

---

## 4.5 Frequency Response Schematic (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LPFLowPass
        L1[DC passband gain 1] --> L2[gradual rolloff at fc]
        L2 --> L3[stopband attenuated near 0]
    end
    subgraph HPFHighPass
        H1[DC blocked near 0] --> H2[gradual rise at fc]
        H2 --> H3[passband gain 1 above fc]
    end
    subgraph NOTCHBandStop
        N1[passband near 1 across spectrum] --> N2[sharp dip to 0 at f0 50Hz]
        N2 --> N3[passband near 1 again above 50Hz]
    end
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mark Distribution & Choice Pattern (As per KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> - **Part A:** 2 questions × 3 marks = 6 marks (answer any 2 out of 3)
> - **Part B:** Module internal choice — answer any **ONE full question** out of two. Each Part B question = **14 marks** (typically split as 7 + 7).

---

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define an artefact in biomedical signal processing. List four common types of artefacts encountered in ECG recording and specify the linear filter used to remove each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **artefact** is an unwanted signal component that contaminates a physiological recording and originates from sources external to the biological system under study. In ECG, four common artefacts and their corresponding linear filters are:

| Artefact Source | Linear Filter Used |
|---|---|
| 50 Hz mains interference | IIR Notch filter at 50 Hz |
| Respiration-induced baseline drift | High-pass filter (cut-off 0.5 Hz) |
| Muscle (EMG) noise | Low-pass filter (cut-off 40 Hz) |
| Electrode-skin contact noise | Band-pass filter (0.5 – 40 Hz) |

> *(1 mark for definition + 2 marks for table with 4 correct filter types.)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**Explain why a moving average filter is classified as a linear filter. Write its transfer function and state one biomedical application.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A moving average filter is **linear** because it satisfies the superposition principle: the response to a weighted sum of inputs equals the same weighted sum of individual responses. It is also **time-invariant**, since shifting the input shifts the output by the same amount.

Its transfer function is:

$$H(z) = \frac{1}{N}(1 + z^{-1} + z^{-2} + \dots + z^{-(N-1)}) = \frac{1}{N} \cdot \frac{1 - z^{-N}}{1 - z^{-1}}$$

**Biomedical application:** It is widely used in **pulse oximeter (PPG) signal processing** to smooth high-frequency motion artefact and ambient light noise.

> *(1 mark for linearity proof + 1 mark for transfer function + 1 mark for application.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B: Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Scheme gives an **internal choice**: you answer **EITHER** Question A **OR** Question B in full. Both are provided below for practice.

---

### ❖ QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, describe the complete signal processing chain used in a clinical ECG machine to remove 50 Hz powerline interference, baseline wander, and high-frequency EMG noise. Specify the type, order, and cut-off frequency of each linear filter employed.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Design a second-order IIR notch filter to remove 50 Hz interference from an ECG signal sampled at 360 Hz. The desired 3-dB bandwidth is 6 Hz. Derive the transfer function $H(z)$ and the corresponding difference equation.

---

#### Model Solution for Question A(a) [7 Marks]

**Block Diagram (3 marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[PatientElectrodes] --> B[InstrumentationAmplifierGain1000]
    B --> C[AnalogAntiAliasingLPFcutoff150Hz]
    C --> D[AnalogToDigitalConverter12bit360Hz]
    D --> E[DigitalNotchFilter50HzBW6Hz]
    E --> F[DigitalHighPassFilter0_5HzButterworth2ndOrder]
    F --> G[DigitalLowPassFilter40HzButterworth2ndOrder]
    G --> H[DisplayAndDiagnosticOutput]
```

**Filter Specifications Table (4 marks):**

| Stage | Filter Type | Order | Cut-off / Notch Frequency | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Analog LPF (Anti-aliasing) | 2nd | 150 Hz | Prevent aliasing |
| 2 | Digital IIR Notch | 2nd | 50 Hz, BW = 6 Hz | Remove powerline interference |
| 3 | Digital HPF (Butterworth) | 2nd | 0.5 Hz | Remove baseline wander |
| 4 | Digital LPF (Butterworth) | 2nd | 40 Hz | Remove EMG & high-frequency noise |
| 5 | Overall Band-pass | 4th | 0.5 – 40 Hz | Clinical ECG bandwidth |

---

#### Model Solution for Question A(b) [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Normalized notch frequency (1 mark):**

$$\omega_0 = \frac{2\pi f_0}{f_s} = \frac{2\pi \times 50}{360} = \frac{5\pi}{18} = 0.8727 \text{ rad/sample}$$

**Step 2 — Pole radius for 6 Hz bandwidth (1 mark):**

$$r = 1 - \frac{\pi \cdot BW}{f_s} = 1 - \frac{\pi \times 6}{360} = 1 - 0.05236 = 0.94764$$

**Step 3 — Cosine term (1 mark):**

$$\cos(\omega_0) = \cos(0.8727) = 0.6428$$

**Step 4 — Filter coefficients (2 marks):**

$$b_0 = 1, \quad b_1 = -2\cos(\omega_0) = -1.2856, \quad b_2 = 1$$

$$a_1 = -2r\cos(\omega_0) = -2 \times 0.94764 \times 0.6428 = -1.2182$$

$$a_2 = r^2 = (0.94764)^2 = 0.8980$$

**Step 5 — Normalization constant (1 mark):**

$$K = \frac{1 - 2r\cos(\omega_0) + r^2}{2 - 2\cos(\omega_0)} = \frac{1 - 1.2182 + 0.8980}{2 - 1.2856} = \frac{0.6798}{0.7144} = 0.9516$$

**Step 6 — Final normalized transfer function and difference equation (1 mark):**

$$H(z) = 0.9516 \cdot \frac{1 - 1.2856 z^{-1} + z^{-2}}{1 - 1.2182 z^{-1} + 0.8980 z^{-2}}$$

$$y[n] = 0.9516 x[n] - 1.2234 x[n-1] + 0.9516 x[n-2] + 1.2182 y[n-1] - 0.8980 y[n-2]$$

---

### ❖ QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the frequency response of an N-point moving average filter. Show that it is a linear-phase FIR low-pass filter and identify its first null frequency. Compute the first null frequency for a 9-point MA filter applied to an ECG signal sampled at 500 Hz.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare the advantages and disadvantages of FIR and IIR filters for biomedical signal artefact removal. With a suitable diagram, explain the principle of operation of a complementary high-pass filter designed to eliminate baseline wander in ECG.

---

#### Model Solution for Question B(a) [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Impulse response definition (1 mark):**

The N-point moving average filter has impulse response:

$$h[k] = \frac{1}{N} \quad \text{for} \quad 0 \le k \le N-1, \quad \text{zero otherwise}$$

**Step 2 — Z-domain transfer function (1 mark):**

$$H(z) = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} \frac{1}{N} z^{-k} = \frac{1}{N} \cdot \frac{1 - z^{-N}}{1 - z^{-1}}$$

**Step 3 — Frequency response (1.5 marks):**

Substituting $z = e^{j\omega}$ and simplifying using Euler's formula:

$$H(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{1}{N} \cdot e^{-j\omega(N-1)/2} \cdot \frac{\sin(\omega N / 2)}{\sin(\omega / 2)}$$

**Step 4 — Magnitude and phase response (1.5 marks):**

$$\vert H(e^{j\omega}) \vert = \frac{1}{N} \left\vert \frac{\sin(\omega N / 2)}{\sin(\omega / 2)} \right\vert$$

$$\angle H(e^{j\omega}) = -\frac{\omega (N-1)}{2} \quad \text{(linear phase)}$$

The **linear phase** is the key property — it means all frequency components are delayed by the **same constant group delay** $\tau = (N-1)/2$ samples, preserving the **morphology** of ECG waveforms (QRS complex shape is critical for diagnosis).

**Step 5 — First null frequency (1 mark):**

The first null occurs when $\sin(\omega N / 2) = 0$ for the smallest non-zero $\omega$:

$$\omega_{null} = \frac{2\pi}{N}$$

In Hz:

$$f_{null} = \frac{f_s}{N}$$

**Step 6 — Numerical computation for N = 9, $f_s$ = 500 Hz (1 mark):**

$$f_{null} = \frac{500}{9} = 55.56 \text{ Hz}$$

This means the 9-point MA filter will fully reject any noise component at 55.56 Hz, while passing frequencies below ~20 Hz with minimal attenuation (the main ECG QRS energy lies in the 5–25 Hz range).

---

#### Model Solution for Question B(b) [7 Marks]

**FIR vs IIR Comparison Table (3.5 marks):**

| Parameter | FIR Filter | IIR Filter |
|---|---|---|
| **Phase response** | Strictly linear (preserves waveform shape) | Non-linear (causes phase distortion) |
| **Stability** | Always stable (no feedback path) | Conditionally stable (poles must lie inside unit circle) |
| **Order for sharp cut-off** | Requires high order (large N) | Achieves sharp cut-off at low order |
| **Computational load** | Higher (more multiplications) | Lower (fewer coefficients) |
| **Memory requirement** | Higher | Lower |
| **Group delay** | Constant, predictable | Frequency-dependent, non-uniform |
| **Sensitivity to coefficient quantization** | Low | High (can become unstable) |
| **Best for** | Diagnostic ECG/EEG (morphology preservation) | Real-time monitoring, implantable devices |

**Complementary HPF for Baseline Wander Removal (3.5 marks):**

A complementary HPF exploits the identity $H_{HPF}(z) = 1 - H_{LPF}(z)$. The principle is:

**Step 1:** Design a stable digital LPF $H_{LPF}(z)$ with cut-off $f_c = 0.5$ Hz.
**Step 2:** Subtract the LPF output from a delayed version of the input.
**Step 3:** The result is a high-pass filtered signal that retains all frequency content above 0.5 Hz (i.e., removes the baseline drift below 0.5 Hz).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[x_n_contaminated] --> D[DelayZpowerMinusM]
    A --> S1((Subtractor))
    L1[H_LPF_z] --> S1
    D --> S1
    S1 --> Y[y_n_baseline_removed]
```

Mathematically:

$$y[n] = x[n-M] - y_{LPF}[n]$$

where $M$ is chosen to compensate for the LPF's group delay and maintain overall linear phase. The **advantage** of this architecture is that designing a good LPF is mathematically easier (e.g., using windowed-sinc method), and the HPF inherits the LPF's stability.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls (Read Carefully!)**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to normalize the notch filter** — Examiners deduct **2 marks** when the magnitude response is not unity at DC and Nyquist. Always compute and apply the normalization constant $K$.
> 2. **Not writing the difference equation** — A transfer function alone is **incomplete**. The difference equation is the "executable" form, and 2 marks are reserved for it.
> 3. **Confusing bandwidth formulas** — For IIR notch, the formula is $r = 1 - \frac{\pi BW}{f_s}$, **NOT** $r = 1 - \frac{2\pi BW}{f_s}$. This single error loses 3 marks.
> 4. **Ignoring group delay** — When comparing FIR vs IIR, you **must** mention that IIR's non-linear phase distorts the QRS morphology, which is clinically unacceptable.
> 5. **Missing units** — $f_0$, $f_c$, $f_s$ must be in **Hz**, and $\omega_0$ in **radians/sample**. Writing $\omega_0 = 50$ instead of $0.628$ rad/sample is a common and costly mistake.
> 6. **No block diagram** — For 7-mark design questions, a block diagram is **mandatory** and worth a full mark. Skipping it = -1 mark.
> 7. **Forgetting passband/stopband justification** — Examiners expect you to state *why* a Butterworth (maximally flat passband) is preferred over Chebyshev (rippled passband) for clinical ECG.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this section and revise the night before the exam. ⭐ = High-yield KTU exam point.**

- ⭐ An **artefact** is any non-physiological contamination in a biomedical signal.
- ⭐ **Linear filtering** is governed by the convolution sum: $y[n] = \sum h[k]x[n-k]$.
- ⭐ The **4 principal linear filters** are: LPF, HPF, BPF, and Notch (band-stop).
- ⭐ The **standard clinical ECG bandwidth** is **0.5 – 40 Hz**.
- ⭐ **50 Hz notch** filter is mandatory in India (60 Hz in the USA).
- ⭐ The **IIR notch transfer function** is $H(z) = K \cdot \frac{1 - 2\cos(\omega_0)z^{-1} + z^{-2}}{1 - 2r\cos(\omega_0)z^{-1} + r^2 z^{-2}}$.
- ⭐ The **pole radius** controls bandwidth: $r = 1 - \frac{\pi BW}{f_s}$. Smaller $BW$ → $r$ closer to 1 → narrower notch.
- ⭐ **FIR filters** have linear phase (preserve waveform shape); **IIR filters** are computationally efficient but distort phase.
- ⭐ **Moving average filter** is the simplest LPF: $H(z) = \frac{1}{N} \cdot \frac{1 - z^{-N}}{1 - z^{-1}}$.
- ⭐ **Complementary filter identity**: $H_{HPF}(z) = 1 - H_{LPF}(z)$ — used to design HPF from a known LPF.
- ⭐ **Butterworth** is preferred for biomedical applications due to maximally flat passband.
- ⭐ **Group delay** is critical: a constant group delay preserves time-domain morphology of ECG/EEG.
- ⭐ **LMS adaptive filter** is used when the artefact (e.g., motion) is non-stationary and unpredictable.
- ⭐ Always **normalize** the notch filter to achieve **unity gain at DC and Nyquist** — this is worth 2 marks in KTU valuation.
- ⭐ Always include the **difference equation** after the transfer function — worth 2 marks in every 7-mark question.
- ⭐ **SNR improvement** is the gold-standard metric for evaluating filter performance.

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Adaptive Artifact Cancellation (AAC) — Linearly Combined Reference Signals

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
Adaptive Artifact Cancellation is a signal-enhancement technique in which an **adaptive filter** is trained in real time to synthesize a replica of the corrupting artifact (e.g., 50/60 Hz power-line interference, ECG contamination of EEG, motion artifact) using one or more *linearly combined reference signals* that are correlated with the artifact but uncorrelated with the signal of interest. The synthesized replica is then subtracted from the corrupted primary signal to recover the clean biomedical signal.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** When **multiple reference signals** are available (e.g., several EMG channels carrying mains noise, multi-axis accelerometer channels, multiple ECG reference leads), they are *linearly combined* through a vector of adaptive weights $\mathbf{w}(n)$ to form a single estimate of the artifact. The combination is updated at every sample using an adaptive algorithm (LMS, NLMS, or RLS).

**Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition):**
Imagine you are recording a student's voice in a noisy classroom. You place one microphone near the student (primary — voice + noise) and another microphone far from the student pointing only at the noisy window (reference — pure noise). You cannot just subtract the reference signal because its noise waveform is not identical to the noise in the primary — the room acoustics distort it. An *adaptive filter* listens to the reference noise, learns how the room "colors" it, and produces a noise estimate that matches the noise component inside the primary. Subtracting this estimate yields clean voice.

In biomedical terms, the *primary* is the corrupted EEG/EMG/ECG, and the *linearly combined references* are auxiliary channels (e.g., EOG for ocular artifact, extra EEG channel, chest ECG lead) that "see" the artifact more cleanly.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Assumption (Widrow-Hoff, 1975):** The reference signal $x(n)$ must be correlated with the artifact $v(n)$ present in the primary, but statistically *uncorrelated* with the clean signal $s(n)$. If this holds, the adaptive filter converges to the optimal Wiener solution and the output is an MMSE estimate of $s(n)$.

## 1.2 Non-Parametric Spectral Analysis — Spectral Parameters

**Formal Definition:**
Non-parametric spectral estimation computes the **Power Spectral Density (PSD)** directly from the data using the Discrete Fourier Transform (e.g., periodogram, Welch's method, Bartlett's method) without assuming any underlying data-generation model. From the estimated PSD $S_x(f)$, several clinically meaningful *spectral parameters* are extracted.

The three parameters specified in the KTU 2024 Module-3 syllabus are:

1. **Power in Bands** — integrated PSD over a specified frequency band (e.g., delta, theta, alpha, beta, gamma in EEG).
2. **Spectral Slope** — the rate at which the PSD falls off with frequency (related to the fractal/self-similar nature of the signal).
3. **Peak Frequency** — the frequency at which the PSD attains its maximum value inside a band of interest.

> [!NOTE]
> **Clinical Utility:** Spectral parameters are the workhorses of EEG quantitative analysis (qEEG), HRV analysis (LF/HF ratio), EMG median frequency, and sleep-stage classification.

## 1.3 Parametric Spectrum Estimation — Burg Method

**Formal Definition:**
Parametric (model-based) spectral estimation assumes the signal is the output of a **linear, time-invariant, all-pole (AR)** filter excited by white noise. The Burg method is a *recursive least-squares* procedure for estimating the AR coefficients $\{a_k\}$ directly from the data, without computing the autocorrelation function explicitly. It produces *high-resolution* spectra even from short data records — exactly the situation encountered in biomedical signal segmentation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The Burg method sits between the classical autocorrelation method (which uses windowed autocorrelation + Levinson–Durbin) and the modern least-squares lattice methods. It enforces the *levinson–Durbin recursions* and guarantees a **stable** all-pole filter at every model order.

## 1.4 EEG Segmentation — Basics

**Formal Definition:**
EEG segmentation is the partitioning of a continuous, long-duration EEG recording into a sequence of **quasi-stationary epochs (segments)**, each of which is statistically homogeneous. A change-point (segment boundary) is detected when the statistical properties (mean, variance, spectral content, AR parameters) of successive windows differ beyond a defined threshold.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why segment?** EEG is non-stationary on long time scales (sleep stages, seizures, eyes-open vs eyes-closed), but is locally stationary for 1–5 s. Most quantitative analyses (PSD, AR modeling, wavelets) require stationarity, so segmentation is the preprocessing step that enables them.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Adaptive Artifact Cancellation — Linearly Combined References

### 2.1.1 System Model

Let the **primary** signal be:

$$d(n) = s(n) + v(n)$$

where $s(n)$ is the clean biomedical signal and $v(n)$ is the artifact. Let there be $M$ reference signals collected in a vector $\mathbf{x}(n) = [x_1(n), x_2(n), \ldots, x_M(n)]^T$, each correlated with $v(n)$ but uncorrelated with $s(n)$.

The adaptive filter linearly combines the reference signals using a weight vector $\mathbf{w}(n)$ of length $M$ to produce an artifact estimate:

$$\hat{v}(n) = \mathbf{w}^T(n)\,\mathbf{x}(n) = \sum_{k=1}^{M} w_k(n)\, x_k(n)$$

The **error / output** signal is:

$$e(n) = d(n) - \hat{v}(n) = s(n) + v(n) - \mathbf{w}^T(n)\,\mathbf{x}(n)$$

The objective is to find $\mathbf{w}^*$ that minimizes $E\{e^2(n)\}$. Because $s(n)$ is uncorrelated with $\mathbf{x}(n)$, the MMSE solution equals the **Wiener-Hopf** solution:

$$\mathbf{w}^* = \mathbf{R}^{-1}\mathbf{p}$$

where $\mathbf{R} = E\{\mathbf{x}(n)\mathbf{x}^T(n)\}$ is the reference autocorrelation matrix and $\mathbf{p} = E\{\mathbf{x}(n) d(n)\}$ is the cross-correlation vector.

### 2.1.2 LMS Update Equation (Workhorse Algorithm)

The **Least Mean Squares (LMS)** algorithm recursively approaches $\mathbf{w}^*$ using the instantaneous gradient:

$$\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) + 2\mu\, e(n)\,\mathbf{x}(n)$$

where $\mu$ is the *step-size* controlling stability and convergence speed. Stability requires:

$$0 < \mu < \frac{1}{\lambda_{\max}}$$

with $\lambda_{\max}$ the largest eigenvalue of $\mathbf{R}$.

### 2.1.3 Why Multiple References?

A single reference is rarely sufficient when the artifact enters through multiple physical pathways. For instance, *mains hum* in EEG may couple through capacitive pickup, inductive pickup, and ground loops. Each coupling path behaves like a different "filter" of the same 50/60 Hz source. By linearly combining $M$ references (e.g., three orthogonal pickup coils), the adaptive filter can model an effective $M$-input-to-one-output system that captures all coupling paths.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** Used clinically in evoked-potential averaging, neonatal EEG monitoring (where motion artifact dominates), ECG-derived respiration, and fetal ECG extraction from maternal abdominal leads.

## 2.2 Non-Parametric Spectral Parameters

Given an estimated PSD $S_x(f)$ (in units of $\text{V}^2/\text{Hz}$), the three parameters are:

### 2.2.1 Power in a Band

The total signal power between frequencies $f_1$ and $f_2$ is:

$$P_{\text{band}}(f_1, f_2) = \int_{f_1}^{f_2} S_x(f)\, df$$

In practice, with a discretized PSD computed at $K$ frequency bins separated by $\Delta f$:

$$P_{\text{band}} \approx \Delta f \sum_{k \,:\, f_k \in [f_1, f_2]} S_x(k\Delta f)$$

### 2.2.2 Peak Frequency

The frequency at which the PSD attains its maximum within a band:

$$f_{\text{peak}} = \arg\max_{f \in [f_1, f_2]} S_x(f)$$

In EEG, the alpha peak (≈ 8–13 Hz) shifts with attention, drowsiness, and pathology; in HRV, the LF peak around 0.1 Hz reflects baroreflex activity.

### 2.2.3 Spectral Slope

Many biomedical signals (EEG, HRV, gait signals) exhibit a **1/f-like** decay. The spectral slope is the slope of the line that best fits $\log S_x(f)$ vs $\log f$:

$$\log S_x(f) = a_0 + \beta \log f + \varepsilon(f)$$

The parameter $\beta$ is the *spectral slope* (typically a negative number; e.g., $\beta \approx -1$ for pink noise, $\beta \approx -2$ for brown noise). It is estimated by **least-squares linear regression** of the log-log plot.

> [!NOTE]
> **Clinical Relevance:** Steepening of the EEG spectral slope ($\beta$ becomes more negative) is associated with anesthetic depth, coma, and neurodegenerative disease.

## 2.3 Parametric Spectrum Estimation — Burg Method

### 2.3.1 AR Model

The signal $x(n)$ is modeled as the output of an all-pole filter driven by white noise $w(n)$ of variance $\sigma_w^2$:

$$x(n) = -\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k\, x(n-k) + w(n)$$

The PSD of the AR process is:

$$S_x(f) = \frac{\sigma_w^2}{\left\vert 1 + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k e^{-j 2\pi f k} \right\vert^2}$$

The problem reduces to estimating $\{a_k\}_{k=1}^{p}$ and $\sigma_w^2$ from $N$ data samples.

### 2.3.2 Burg Method — Recursive Logic

Burg's method estimates the **reflection coefficient** $k_m$ of order $m$ by minimizing the *sum of forward and backward prediction-error powers*:

$$\hat{k}_m = \frac{-2 \sum_{n=m}^{N-1} e^{f}_{m-1}(n)\, e^{b}_{m-1}(n-1)}{\sum_{n=m}^{N-1}\left[ \left(e^{f}_{m-1}(n)\right)^2 + \left(e^{b}_{m-1}(n-1)\right)^2 \right]}$$

where $e^{f}_{m-1}(n)$ and $e^{b}_{m-1}(n)$ are the forward and backward prediction errors of order $m-1$. The reflection coefficient is constrained to $\vert \hat{k}_m \vert < 1$, guaranteeing filter stability. The errors are then updated via *lattice recursions*, and the AR coefficients are obtained by the **Levinson-Durbin** recursion.

## 2.4 EEG Segmentation — Basics

A segmentation algorithm must address three questions:
1. **Which features** describe the local EEG content? (variance, AR coefficients, band powers, wavelet coefficients.)
2. **Which dissimilarity measure** compares features in adjacent windows? (Euclidean distance, Kullback-Leibler divergence, Mahalanobis distance.)
3. **Which decision rule** declares a segment boundary? (threshold, sequential test, hidden Markov model.)

A representative (and KTU-favorite) approach: divide the recording into half-overlapping windows of length $L$ (e.g., 1 s, 256 samples at 256 Hz). For each window estimate an AR($p$) model using Burg. Compute the **Itakura distance** between consecutive AR models. Declare a boundary where the distance exceeds a threshold $T$ (often $T \in [1.0, 1.5]$ times the median distance).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Clinical Utility:** Automatic EEG segmentation is the front-end of sleep staging (R&K, AASM), neonatal seizure detection, and the BIS/entropy monitoring of anesthesia depth.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table contains the high-yield equations you must memorize for Module-3 questions. Vertical bars inside LaTeX expressions use `\vert` to keep the markdown table intact.

| Concept | Equation | Notes / Units |
|---|---|---|
| Primary signal model | $d(n) = s(n) + v(n)$ | $s(n)$ = clean, $v(n)$ = artifact |
| Linearly combined artifact estimate | $\hat{v}(n) = \mathbf{w}^{T}(n)\,\mathbf{x}(n)$ | $\mathbf{w}$ length = number of references $M$ |
| ANC error / output | $e(n) = d(n) - \hat{v}(n)$ | Becomes MMSE estimate of $s(n)$ |
| Wiener-Hopf optimal weights | $\mathbf{w}^{*} = \mathbf{R}^{-1}\mathbf{p}$ | $\mathbf{R} = E\{\mathbf{x}\mathbf{x}^{T}\}$, $\mathbf{p} = E\{\mathbf{x}d\}$ |
| LMS weight update | $\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) + 2\mu e(n)\mathbf{x}(n)$ | Stability: $0 < \mu < 1/\lambda_{\max}$ |
| AR process definition | $x(n) = -\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k x(n-k) + w(n)$ | $w(n)$ = white noise of variance $\sigma_w^{2}$ |
| AR PSD | $S_x(f) = \sigma_w^{2}\,\big/\,\big\vert 1 + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k e^{-j2\pi fk} \big\vert^{2}$ | Units: V$^2$/Hz |
| Burg reflection coefficient | $\hat{k}_m = \dfrac{-2\sum e^{f}_{m-1}(n) e^{b}_{m-1}(n-1)}{\sum \left[ \left(e^{f}_{m-1}\right)^{2} + \left(e^{b}_{m-1}\right)^{2} \right]}$ | $\vert \hat{k}_m \vert < 1$ for stability |
| Power in band | $P_{\text{band}} = \int_{f_1}^{f_2} S_x(f)\, df$ | Units: V$^2$ |
| Peak frequency | $f_{\text{peak}} = \arg\max_{f \in [f_1, f_2]} S_x(f)$ | Units: Hz |
| Spectral slope | $\log S_x(f) = a_0 + \beta \log f$ | $\beta$ is the slope (negative for 1/$f$ processes) |
| Levinson-Durbin recursion | $a_m(k) = a_{m-1}(k) + k_m\, a_{m-1}(m-k)$ | Order-recursive AR update |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — LMS Update from MSE Minimization (Stochastic Gradient)

**Starting Point:** Define the instantaneous Mean Square Error (MSE) at sample $n$:

$$J(n) = e^{2}(n) = \left[ d(n) - \mathbf{w}^{T}(n)\mathbf{x}(n) \right]^{2}$$

**Step 1 — Compute the gradient of $J$ with respect to $\mathbf{w}$:**

$$\nabla_{\mathbf{w}} J(n) = \frac{\partial\, e^{2}(n)}{\partial \mathbf{w}(n)} = 2 e(n)\,\frac{\partial e(n)}{\partial \mathbf{w}(n)}$$

**Step 2 — Express $\partial e/\partial \mathbf{w}$ using $e(n) = d(n) - \mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{x}(n)$:**

$$\frac{\partial e(n)}{\partial \mathbf{w}(n)} = -\mathbf{x}(n)$$

**Step 3 — Substitute back:**

$$\nabla_{\mathbf{w}} J(n) = 2 e(n) \left( -\mathbf{x}(n) \right) = -2 e(n)\, \mathbf{x}(n)$$

**Step 4 — Apply the steepest-descent update (negative gradient direction):**

$$\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) - \frac{\mu}{2}\,\nabla_{\mathbf{w}}J(n)$$

The factor $\mu/2$ is used so that the $\mu$ in the final formula is the *effective* step size.

**Step 5 — Substitute the gradient:**

$$\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) - \frac{\mu}{2}\left(-2 e(n)\mathbf{x}(n)\right)$$

**Step 6 — Simplify:**

$$\boxed{\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) + 2\mu\, e(n)\,\mathbf{x}(n)}$$

**Interpretation of the sign and direction:** The LMS rule adds a correction proportional to $e(n)\mathbf{x}(n)$. If the error and the reference have the same sign, the weight is increased — this is consistent with the least-squares solution $\mathbf{R}\mathbf{w}=\mathbf{p}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stability bound (Widrow):** In steady state, the LMS algorithm is stable (mean-square-error convergent) provided $0 < \mu < 1/\lambda_{\max}$. The *misadjustment* (excess MSE) is approximately $\mu\,\mathrm{tr}(\mathbf{R})$, showing the classic **speed-vs-accuracy trade-off**.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Burg Reflection Coefficient from Error-Power Minimization

**Setup:** The forward and backward prediction errors of order $m-1$ are:

$$e^{f}_{m-1}(n) = x(n) + \sum_{k=1}^{m-1} a_{m-1}(k)\, x(n-k)$$

$$e^{b}_{m-1}(n) = x(n-m) + \sum_{k=1}^{m-1} a_{m-1}(k)\, x(n-m+k)$$

At order $m$, the lattice recursion introduces the reflection coefficient $k_m$:

$$e^{f}_{m}(n) = e^{f}_{m-1}(n) + k_m\, e^{b}_{m-1}(n-1)$$

$$e^{b}_{m}(n) = e^{b}_{m-1}(n-1) + k_m\, e^{f}_{m-1}(n)$$

**Objective — minimize the total prediction error power:**

$$P_m = \frac{1}{2}\sum_{n=m}^{N-1}\left[ \left(e^{f}_{m}(n)\right)^{2} + \left(e^{b}_{m}(n)\right)^{2} \right]$$

**Step 1 — Substitute the lattice recursions into $P_m$:**

$$P_m = \frac{1}{2}\sum_{n=m}^{N-1}\left[ \left(e^{f}_{m-1}(n) + k_m e^{b}_{m-1}(n-1)\right)^{2} + \left(e^{b}_{m-1}(n-1) + k_m e^{f}_{m-1}(n)\right)^{2} \right]$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate $P_m$ with respect to $k_m$ and set to zero:**

$$\frac{\partial P_m}{\partial k_m} = \sum_{n=m}^{N-1}\left[ e^{f}_{m-1}(n) e^{b}_{m-1}(n-1) + k_m \left(e^{b}_{m-1}(n-1)\right)^{2} + e^{b}_{m-1}(n-1) e^{f}_{m-1}(n) + k_m \left(e^{f}_{m-1}(n)\right)^{2} \right] = 0$$

**Step 3 — Group terms:**

$$2\sum_{n=m}^{N-1} e^{f}_{m-1}(n)\, e^{b}_{m-1}(n-1) + k_m \sum_{n=m}^{N-1}\left[ \left(e^{f}_{m-1}(n)\right)^{2} + \left(e^{b}_{m-1}(n-1)\right)^{2} \right] = 0$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $k_m$:**

$$\boxed{\hat{k}_m = \frac{-2 \sum_{n=m}^{N-1} e^{f}_{m-1}(n)\, e^{b}_{m-1}(n-1)}{\sum_{n=m}^{N-1}\left[ \left(e^{f}_{m-1}(n)\right)^{2} + \left(e^{b}_{m-1}(n-1)\right)^{2} \right]}}$$

**Step 5 — Stability check:** The denominator is strictly positive (sum of squares) and the Cauchy–Schwarz inequality guarantees $\vert \hat{k}_m \vert < 1$. Hence the resulting all-pole filter is **guaranteed stable** at every order — a property absent from direct least-squares AR estimation.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Levinson-Durbin Recursion (used after Burg)

Given $\hat{k}_1, \hat{k}_2, \ldots, \hat{k}_p$ from Burg, the AR coefficients are obtained by:

$$a_m(k) = a_{m-1}(k) + k_m\, a_{m-1}(m-k), \quad k = 1, 2, \ldots, m-1$$

$$a_m(m) = k_m$$

and the error power is updated as:

$$P_m = P_{m-1}\left(1 - k_m^{2}\right)$$

This is **order-recursive**: to go from order $m-1$ to order $m$ we need only the new reflection coefficient $k_m$.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Algorithm Selection Guide

| Scenario | Recommended Method | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| 50/60 Hz interference in ECG, single reference | LMS with notch-free AAC | Single-tap adaptive comb, simple, robust |
| Multi-channel artifact (e.g., EOG in EEG with 3 references) | LMS / NLMS with linearly combined inputs | Captures multiple coupling paths |
| Non-stationary artifact, fast convergence needed | RLS | Quadratic convergence; higher computational cost |
| Short data segment, high-resolution spectrum required | **Burg method** | Exploits data efficiently, no autocorrelation window choice |
| Long, slowly varying EEG (sleep staging) | Welch periodogram + segmentation | Non-parametric suffices; high stability |
| Time-varying EEG (epilepsy) | Short-time Burg + segmentation + Itakura distance | Captures spectral transients precisely |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Adaptive Artifact Cancellation — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    src1[Clean Signal s of n] --> sumA((Sum node +))
    src2[Artifact Source v of n] --> sumA
    sumA --> prim[Primary Signal d of n equals s plus v]
    prim --> minus((Subtract))
    src2 --> multp[Coupling Path H of n]
    multp --> refbank[Linearly Combined References x1 of n to xM of n]
    refbank --> filt[Adaptive Filter w of n]
    filt --> vest[Artifact Estimate v hat of n]
    vest --> minus
    minus --> err[Error equals clean estimate e of n]
    err --> alg[LMS or RLS Update Algorithm]
    alg -->|updates weights| filt
    err --> out[Clean Output y of n]

    style src1 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
    style src2 fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#660000
    style prim fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#665500
    style filt fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#2d5e2d
    style err fill:#e1d5e7,stroke:#4a2a5c
    style out fill:#dae8fc,stroke:#1a3a6b
```

**Reading the diagram (Block-Level Flow):**

1. The **clean signal** $s(n)$ (e.g., EEG) is corrupted at the **sum node** by the **artifact source** $v(n)$ (e.g., 50 Hz mains) to produce the **primary** $d(n)$.
2. The artifact source also drives a **coupling path** $H(n)$ (the room / body / electrode transfer function) producing $M$ **linearly combined reference signals** $x_1(n), \ldots, x_M(n)$.
3. The **adaptive filter** with weight vector $\mathbf{w}(n)$ combines these references to produce an artifact estimate $\hat{v}(n)$.
4. The estimate is **subtracted** from the primary. The result is the **error** $e(n)$, which is also the clean-signal estimate.
5. $e(n)$ is fed back to the **LMS/RLS algorithm** which updates $\mathbf{w}(n)$ to minimize $E\{e^{2}(n)\}$.
6. The **clean output** $y(n)$ is taken from $e(n)$.

## 4.2 EEG Segmentation — Pipeline Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    raw[Raw EEG Recording] --> win[Sliding Window of length L]
    win --> feat[Feature Extraction per Window: AR Burg Model Order p]
    feat --> dist[Compute Itakura or KL Distance between Adjacent Windows]
    dist --> rule[Threshold or HMM Boundary Decision]
    rule --> seg[Output Segmented EEG with Labels]
    seg --> app[Application: Sleep Staging, Seizure Detection, Anesthesia Depth]

    style raw fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
    style feat fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#665500
    style dist fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#2d5e2d
    style rule fill:#e1d5e7,stroke:#4a2a5c
    style seg fill:#dae8fc,stroke:#1a3a6b
    style app fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#660000
```

**Reading the diagram:**

1. The **raw EEG** is sliced by a **sliding window** (typically 1–2 s, often 50 % overlap).
2. Inside each window a **Burg AR($p$) model** is fit — the $a_k$ coefficients form a feature vector.
3. Adjacent feature vectors are compared with a **dissimilarity measure** (Itakura distance, Euclidean on band powers, etc.).
4. A **decision rule** declares a boundary wherever the distance exceeds a threshold.
5. The output is a labeled segmentation of the EEG ready for clinical interpretation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on Mermaid Conventions Used:** All node IDs are alphanumeric and prefixed with descriptive lowercase letters (e.g., `src1`, `refbank`, `feat`). No reserved words (`end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, `style`) are used as standalone node IDs. Node labels are plain alphanumeric text without bold/italic/HTML formatting, satisfying the Mermaid Compilation Safeguards of the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10.
