## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]**
*Define Kinesiology. List any four major bio-signals used in kinesiological analysis.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Kinesiology is the scientific study of human movement, integrating anatomy, biomechanics, neuromuscular physiology, and signal processing to quantify kinematics (motion) and kinetics (forces) of the musculoskeletal system.

Four major bio-signals used in kinesiology are:
1. **Electromyography (EMG)** — captures muscle electrical activity.
2. **Electroencephalography (EEG)** — captures cortical motor intent.
3. **Goniometer / IMU signals** — capture joint angular displacement and angular velocity.
4. **Force plate signals** — capture Ground Reaction Forces (GRF) during gait.

*(1 mark for definition, ½ mark each for the four signals = total 3 marks.)*

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]**
*What is the Median Frequency (MDF) of an EMG signal? Why is it preferred over Mean Power Frequency (MPF) for muscle fatigue analysis?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
**MDF** is the frequency value $f_{MDF}$ that divides the power spectral density $P(f)$ of the EMG signal into two equal-energy halves, satisfying $\int_{0}^{f_{MDF}} P(f)\, df = \dfrac{1}{2}\int_{0}^{\infty} P(f)\, df$.

MDF is preferred over MPF for muscle fatigue analysis because:
1. **Robustness to noise**: MDF depends on the *cumulative distribution* of power, not on a weighted mean; hence, random noise spikes in isolated frequency bins do not shift MDF as drastically.
2. **Robustness to cross-talk**: Adjacent muscle cross-talk tends to bias the MPF upward, but MDF remains relatively stable.
3. **Physiological validity**: During sustained isometric contractions, MDF exhibits a smooth, monotonic decline with fatigue, matching the underlying motor unit recruitment dynamics.

*(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for justification = total 3 marks.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**: The most common mark-deduction points in Kinesiology questions are: (a) Confusing *kinematic* with *kinetic* quantities. (b) Forgetting to specify **units** in numerical answers. (c) Using $\vert$ (vertical bar) instead of $\vert \cdot \vert$ notation for absolute value, leading to ambiguity. (d) Skipping the **derivation steps** and jumping to the final formula — KTU examiners allocate 50–60% of the marks to intermediate algebraic transitions. (e) Failing to mention the **validity range** of Hill's constants or the **bandwidth** of EMG signals.

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam - Sample Paper Style, Module 4]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the end-to-end signal processing chain for surface Electromyography (sEMG) used in kinesiological studies, from muscle activation to clinical decision. List the key features extracted in the time and frequency domains. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A subject walks on a force plate at constant speed. The vertical Ground Reaction Force (GRF) recorded has a double-hump pattern with peaks at **120% BW** (first peak) and **110% BW** (second peak), separated by a trough at **75% BW** during mid-stance. If the subject's body mass is **70 kg** and gravitational acceleration is $g = 9.81$ m/s², compute:
   (i) The first peak vertical GRF in Newtons.  
   (ii) The mid-stance vertical GRF in Newtons.  
   (iii) The net vertical acceleration of the center of mass at the first peak, assuming only the vertical GRF and body weight act on the CoM. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

The sEMG signal processing chain comprises the following sequential stages (Block Diagram for reference):

1. **Physiological Source** — Motor unit action potentials propagate along muscle fibers.
2. **Acquisition** — Surface Ag/AgCl electrodes (typically bipolar configuration, inter-electrode distance 20 mm) detect the sum of motor unit action potentials.
3. **Amplification** — Instrumentation amplifier with gain **1000 – 5000** and high CMRR (> **100 dB**).
4. **Filtering** — Bandpass **20 – 500 Hz** (Butterworth, 4th order) and **50 Hz** notch.
5. **Sampling** — ADC at $f_s =$ **1 – 4 kHz** to satisfy the Nyquist criterion.
6. **Feature Extraction**:
   - *Time-domain*: RMS, MAV, WL, ZC.
   - *Frequency-domain*: MPF, MDF.
   - *Time-frequency*: STFT, CWT.
7. **Classification/Interpretation** — KNN, SVM, LDA, or Deep Learning.

*(Block diagram = 2 marks; explanation of each block = 3 marks; feature listing = 2 marks.)*

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Given:** Body mass $m = 70$ kg, $g = 9.81$ m/s², hence body weight $W = mg = 70 \times 9.81 = 686.7$ N.

**Step (i) — First peak vertical GRF:**

$$F_{z,peak1} = 1.20 \times W = 1.20 \times 686.7 = 824.04 \text{ N}$$

*(1 mark for formula, 1 mark for numerical evaluation.)*

**Step (ii) — Mid-stance vertical GRF:**

$$F_{z,trough} = 0.75 \times W = 0.75 \times 686.7 = 515.025 \text{ N}$$

*(1 mark for formula, 1 mark for numerical evaluation.)*

**Step (iii) — Net vertical acceleration of CoM at first peak:**

Applying Newton's second law in the vertical direction:

$$\sum F_z = m \cdot \ddot{z}$$

The net vertical force is the GRF minus the body weight (since weight acts downward):

$$F_{net} = F_{z,peak1} - W = 824.04 - 686.7 = 137.34 \text{ N (upward)}$$

Therefore the vertical acceleration is:

$$\ddot{z} = \dfrac{F_{net}}{m} = \dfrac{137.34}{70} \approx 1.962 \text{ m/s}^2 \text{ (upward)}$$

*(2 marks for Newton's law setup, 1 mark for final numerical value with units.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam - Sample Paper Style, Module 4]**

**(a)** Define the Gait Cycle. With a labeled diagram, describe its phases and the events (Heel Strike, Foot Flat, Mid-Stance, Heel Off, Toe Off) that mark the transitions. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the Symmetry Index (SI) for gait analysis from first principles. A hemiplegic patient has the following stride times: left leg $T_L = 1.42$ s, right leg $T_R = 1.05$ s. Calculate the SI and comment on the clinical interpretation. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

The **Gait Cycle** is the time interval between two successive occurrences of the same gait event of the same foot — typically defined from one **Heel Strike (HS)** of the right foot to the next **Heel Strike** of the right foot.

**Phases of the Gait Cycle (Total: 100%, equivalent to 1.0 stride):**

1. **Stance Phase (≈ 60%)** — The foot is in contact with the ground.
   - **Initial Double Support (0–12%)** — Both feet are in contact; begins at Heel Strike.
   - **Loading Response (0–12%)** — Weight acceptance, foot flat occurs.
   - **Mid-Stance (12–31%)** — Single limb support begins; body CoM passes over the supporting foot.
   - **Terminal Stance (31–50%)** — Heel Off occurs; progression over the forefoot.
   - **Pre-Swing / Terminal Double Support (50–60%)** — Toe Off occurs; the second foot has now made contact.

2. **Swing Phase (≈ 40%)** — The foot is off the ground.
   - **Initial Swing (60–73%)** — Foot clearance begins.
   - **Mid-Swing (73–87%)** — Swing leg passes the stance leg.
   - **Terminal Swing (87–100%)** — Deceleration; next Heel Strike prepares.

*(3 marks for phase definitions with percentages, 2 marks for labeling the events HS, FF, MS, HO, TO, 2 marks for diagram description.)*

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Derivation of the Symmetry Index:**

The Symmetry Index (SI) quantifies the degree of left–right asymmetry in gait. Let $T_L$ be the left-stride duration and $T_R$ be the right-stride duration. The mean reference stride time is:

$$\bar{T} = \dfrac{T_L + T_R}{2}$$

The absolute deviation from symmetry is:

$$\Delta T = \vert T_L - T_R \vert$$

The Symmetry Index, expressed as a percentage, is the ratio of the deviation to the mean:

$$SI = \dfrac{\vert T_L - T_R \vert}{\bar{T}} \times 100\%$$

Substituting the expression for $\bar{T}$:

$$SI = \dfrac{\vert T_L - T_R \vert}{\dfrac{1}{2}(T_L + T_R)} \times 100\%$$

*(3 marks for derivation.)*

**Numerical Computation:**

**Given:** $T_L = 1.42$ s, $T_R = 1.05$ s.

**Step 1 — Compute the mean:**

$$\bar{T} = \dfrac{1.42 + 1.05}{2} = \dfrac{2.47}{2} = 1.235 \text{ s}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the absolute difference:**

$$\Delta T = \vert 1.42 - 1.05 \vert = 0.37 \text{ s}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Symmetry Index:**

$$SI = \dfrac{0.37}{1.235} \times 100\% \approx 29.96\%$$

**Clinical Interpretation:**
An SI of ≈ **30%** is far above the clinically significant threshold of **15%**, indicating **severe left–right gait asymmetry** consistent with the patient's hemiplegic condition. The left (affected) leg has a substantially longer stride time, suggesting compensatory slow walking and reduced motor control on the hemiplegic side. Therapeutic intervention (Bobath/NDT therapy, FES-assisted gait training) is strongly indicated.

*(2 marks for numerical computation, 2 marks for clinical interpretation.)*

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Kinesiology** = interdisciplinary study of human movement, integrating **EMG, EEG, goniometry, accelerometry, and force plate** data.
- ✅ Surface EMG bandwidth is **20–500 Hz**; sampling rate must be **≥ 1 kHz** (Nyquist).
- ✅ The **Median Frequency (MDF)** divides the PSD into two equal-energy halves and is **more robust** to noise than the **Mean Power Frequency (MPF)**.
- ✅ **Hill's muscle model** constants $a$ and $b$ are typically $a \approx T_0/4$ to $T_0/5$ and $b \approx v_{max}/4$ to $v_{max}/5$.
- ✅ **Gait cycle** = Stance (60%) + Swing (40%); cadence is typically **90 – 120 steps/min** in healthy adults.
- ✅ **Symmetry Index** > 15% = clinically significant asymmetry (hemiparesis marker).
- ✅ **GRF peaks** during normal walking = **1.1 – 1.2 × body weight**; running can reach **2.5 – 3.0 × body weight**.
- ✅ **Time-domain features** (RMS, MAV) are computationally cheap → preferred for **real-time prosthetic control**.
- ✅ **Time-frequency features** (CWT, WPD) are essential for **non-stationary dynamic contractions**.
- ✅ Always specify **units** and **validity ranges** in your final answer — failure to do so is a common 1-mark deduction.
- ✅ KTU examiners allocate 50–60% of the marks for **derivations** to intermediate algebraic steps — never jump to the final formula.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ergonomics. Prosthesis Control. Amplitude Estimation in the Surface EMG - Signal Model and ML Estimation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT755 - ADVANCED BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Ergonomics. Prosthesis Control. Amplitude Estimation in the Surface EMG - Signal Model and ML Estimation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Ergonomics in Biomedical Signal Processing

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology):**
Ergonomics in the context of biomedical signal processing is the *human-factors engineering discipline* concerned with the optimal design of bioelectric acquisition systems, electrode interfaces, and prosthetic control schemes such that physiological, cognitive, and biomechanical limitations of the human user are respected while maximizing signal fidelity, system reliability, and intuitive control.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Ergonomics in Module 4 emphasizes the *closed-loop interaction* between the user's residual neuromuscular system, the EMG electrode interface, the digital signal processor, and the external actuator (e.g., prosthetic hand). The ergonomic design metric is the **Usability Index (UI)**, a composite of task accuracy, response latency, and user-reported comfort.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Imagine driving a car with a steering wheel that is either too stiff or too loose. If the wheel is too stiff (high force required), your arms tire within minutes; if it is too loose, you cannot control the car precisely. *Ergonomics finds the "Goldilocks zone"*. In EMG-based prosthetics, ergonomics finds the balance between **signal clarity** (clean electrodes) and **user comfort** (lightweight, non-irritating, sweat-tolerant), and between **controller responsiveness** (low-latency DSP) and **controller intuitiveness** (proportional mapping from EMG amplitude to actuator speed).

Standard metrics used:
- **EMG Amplitude Range:** 0.05 mV to 5 mV (typical surface EMG, voluntary contraction).
- **Usable Bandwidth:** 20 Hz to 500 Hz (dominant energy).
- **Skin-Electrode Impedance Target:** < 5 kΩ at 50 Hz.
- **Maximum Acceptable Latency for Prosthetic Control:** **< 300 ms** (clinical benchmark).

---

## 1.2 Prosthesis Control

**Formal Definition:**
Prosthesis control refers to the *algorithmic mapping* of bioelectric signals (predominantly surface EMG, sEMG) recorded from the residual limb of an amputee into command signals that drive the actuators of an artificial limb (e.g., motor-driven hand, elbow, knee).

**Classification of Prosthesis Control Schemes (KTU 2024 Module 4):**

| Control Paradigm | Input Signal | Decision Logic | Application Class |
|---|---|---|---|
| On-Off Control | Single-channel sEMG amplitude | Threshold-crossing | Simple 1-DOF hands |
| Proportional Control | Single-channel sEMG amplitude | Linear map of amplitude to speed/force | 1-DOF powered limbs |
| Pattern Recognition (PR) Control | Multi-channel sEMG feature vector | LDA / SVM / MLP classifier | Multi-DOF hands |
| Regression-Based Control | Multi-channel sEMG | Continuous mapping to joint angle/torque | Advanced arms/legs |
| Adaptive Control | sEMG + mechanical sensors | Online parameter update | Smart prosthetics |

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Think of prosthesis control as a *language translator*. The "source language" is the language of muscles (firing patterns of motor units), and the "target language" is the language of motors (voltage, current, torque). The DSP block is the *dictionary* that translates one into the other. A good translator preserves *meaning* and *nuance* — that is the ergonomic goal.

---

## 1.3 Amplitude Estimation in Surface EMG

**Formal Definition:**
Amplitude estimation in surface EMG is the process of inferring the underlying *level of muscle activation* (typically expressed as the standard deviation or RMS of the motor-unit action potential train) from a finite, noise-corrupted sEMG observation window.

**Why is it needed?**
- The instantaneous sEMG samples oscillate rapidly (zero-mean) and are not directly proportional to muscle force.
- The *envelope* (or amplitude) of sEMG is monotonically related to voluntary contraction level.
- In prosthesis control, the amplitude estimate is the *control variable* (e.g., grip force setpoint).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Distinction:** The sEMG signal is *stochastic*, not deterministic. Therefore, "amplitude" is a *parameter* (not a sample value) and must be **estimated** using statistical inference — hence the role of **Maximum Likelihood (ML) Estimation**.

## 1.4 Signal Model and ML Estimation — Overview

The amplitude estimation problem is cast as: *Given a finite observation window of sEMG, find the parameter A that makes the observed data most probable.*

**Standard KTU Signal Model:**
$$x(n) = A \cdot s(n) + w(n), \quad n = 0, 1, \ldots, N-1$$

where:
- $x(n)$ = observed sEMG sample at discrete time $n$.
- $A$ = **unknown deterministic amplitude parameter** to be estimated.
- $s(n)$ = known zero-mean, unit-variance *shape function* (e.g., normalized MUAP template). In the simplest case $s(n) = 1$, $A$ is the constant mean level.
- $w(n) \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$ = independent, identically distributed (i.i.d.) **Additive White Gaussian Noise** representing electrode, motion artifact, and electronic noise.
- $N$ = number of samples in the observation window.

**Intuitive Analogy:** Picture a dimmer switch. The wall outlet supplies a *noisy AC signal*. The dimmer knob sets the "amplitude" (parameter A). Your eye observes the *average brightness* over a few seconds (the samples). You have to estimate *how high the dimmer is set* despite flicker noise. ML estimation is the formal statistical recipe for that estimate.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Ergonomics — Underlying Principles

The ergonomic design of EMG-based systems rests on three pillars:

1. **Anatomical Compatibility**
   - Electrode size: standard disc electrode $\approx$ **10 mm diameter**; smaller (5 mm) for high spatial resolution at cost of SNR.
   - Inter-electrode distance (IED): typically **20 mm** along the muscle fibre direction.
   - Electrode placement: on the muscle belly, between motor point and tendon.

2. **Cognitive Compatibility**
   - Mapping of EMG patterns to actuator behaviour must be **learnable** within a clinically reasonable training time (typically < 30 minutes per pattern).
   - Feedback modality: vibrotactile, audio, or visual confirmation of control intent.

3. **Biomechanical Compatibility**
   - Mass of socket-mounted electronics: target **< 300 g** for upper-limb prosthetic.
   - Power autonomy: target **> 8 hours** of continuous use per charge.

## 2.2 Prosthesis Control — Operational Logic

### Step 1: Acquisition
Differential sEMG is acquired from $M$ electrode pairs (typically $M = 2$ to $M = 8$). The signal is bandpass filtered (**20–500 Hz**), notch-filtered at 50/60 Hz, and sampled at $f_s \geq 1$ kHz (Nyquist criterion).

### Step 2: Windowing
A sliding analysis window of length $N$ (typically $N = 100$ to $N = 300$ samples, i.e., 100–300 ms at $f_s = 1$ kHz) is applied. Window advancement step is typically $N/2$ to $N/3$ (50%–66% overlap).

### Step 3: Feature Extraction
For each window, a feature vector is computed. The most common amplitude-related features are:
- **Mean Absolute Value (MAV):** $\displaystyle \text{MAV} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} \vert x(n) \vert$
- **Root Mean Square (RMS):** $\displaystyle \text{RMS} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x^2(n)}$
- **Variance:** $\displaystyle \sigma_x^2 = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} (x(n) - \bar{x})^2$
- **Waveform Length, Zero Crossings, Slope Sign Changes** (used in PR control).

### Step 4: Decision / Mapping
The amplitude feature is mapped to an actuator command:
- *Proportional control:* $u = K \cdot \text{RMS}$ where $K$ is the user-tuned gain.
- *Threshold control:* $u = 0$ if $\text{RMS} < \tau_{\text{on}}}$; $u = U_{\max}$ if $\text{RMS} > \tau_{\text{off}}$.

## 2.3 ML Estimation — Theory

### 2.3.1 The Likelihood Function

Given i.i.d. observations $\{x(0), x(1), \ldots, x(N-1)\}$ drawn from the model $x(n) = A + w(n)$ with $w(n) \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$ and known $\sigma^2$:

$$L(A) = \prod_{n=0}^{N-1} f_{x(n)}(x(n); A) = \prod_{n=0}^{N-1} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{(x(n) - A)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$$

$$L(A) = \left(\frac{1}{2\pi\sigma^2}\right)^{N/2} \exp\!\left(-\frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1}(x(n) - A)^2\right)$$

### 2.3.2 The Log-Likelihood Function

Taking the natural logarithm (a monotonically increasing transformation, so the maximizer is unchanged):

$$\ln L(A) = -\frac{N}{2}\ln(2\pi\sigma^2) - \frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1}(x(n) - A)^2$$

### 2.3.3 Maximization — The ML Estimate

Differentiate with respect to $A$ and set to zero:

$$\frac{\partial \ln L(A)}{\partial A} = \frac{1}{\sigma^2}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1}(x(n) - A) = 0$$

Solving:

$$\boxed{\;\hat{A}_{\text{ML}} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n) = \bar{x}\;}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** When the noise is Gaussian and the signal is a constant $A$, the **ML estimate coincides with the sample mean**, which is also the *Minimum Variance Unbiased Estimator* (MVUE) in this case. This is a *classical* result in estimation theory and a frequent KTU exam question.

### 2.3.4 The General Case with Known Shape $s(n)$

For $x(n) = A \cdot s(n) + w(n)$ with known $s(n)$ and $w(n) \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$:

$$\hat{A}_{\text{ML}} = \frac{\displaystyle\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} s(n)\, x(n)}{\displaystyle\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} s^2(n)}$$

This is the **normalized correlator output** — the optimal detector in Gaussian noise.

### 2.3.5 Cramér–Rao Lower Bound (CRLB)

For the constant-amplitude case, the CRLB is:

$$\text{var}(\hat{A}) \geq \frac{\sigma^2}{N}$$

The ML estimator achieves the CRLB for this model (it is *efficient*), so:

$$\text{var}(\hat{A}_{\text{ML}}) = \frac{\sigma^2}{N}, \quad \text{std}(\hat{A}_{\text{ML}}) = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{N}}$$

This is the *standard error of the mean* — a quantity that decreases as $1/\sqrt{N}$, justifying the use of longer analysis windows for better amplitude precision, subject to the *stationarity* requirement (window must remain within a single contraction level).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | sEMG Signal Model (constant amplitude) | $x(n) = A + w(n)$ | $w(n) \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\sigma^2)$ |
| 2 | sEMG Signal Model (general) | $x(n) = A\,s(n) + w(n)$ | $s(n)$ zero-mean, unit-variance |
| 3 | Likelihood Function | $L(A) = (2\pi\sigma^2)^{-N/2}\exp\!\big(-\frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\sum (x(n)-A)^2\big)$ | Product of Gaussian PDFs |
| 4 | Log-Likelihood | $\ln L = -\frac{N}{2}\ln(2\pi\sigma^2) - \frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\sum (x(n)-A)^2$ | Monotone in $A$ |
| 5 | ML Estimate (constant case) | $\hat{A}_{\text{ML}} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n)$ | Equals the sample mean $\bar{x}$ |
| 6 | ML Estimate (shape $s(n)$ case) | $\hat{A}_{\text{ML}} = \frac{\sum s(n)x(n)}{\sum s^2(n)}$ | Correlator / matched filter |
| 7 | CRLB (constant case) | $\text{var}(\hat{A}) \geq \sigma^2/N$ | Achieved with equality by ML |
| 8 | Standard Error of $\hat{A}_{\text{ML}}$ | $\text{SE} = \sigma/\sqrt{N}$ | Decreases with $\sqrt{N}$ |
| 9 | RMS Feature | $\text{RMS} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N}\sum x^2(n)}$ | Common amplitude feature |
| 10 | MAV Feature | $\text{MAV} = \frac{1}{N}\sum \vert x(n) \vert$ | Common amplitude feature |
| 11 | Signal-to-Noise Ratio (amplitude) | $\text{SNR} = A^2/\sigma^2$ | Power-domain definition |
| 12 | Minimum Latency (prosthetic) | $L_{\min} \leq$ **300 ms** | Clinical ergonomic benchmark |
| 13 | Usable sEMG Bandwidth | **20 Hz – 500 Hz** | Standard filter passband |
| 14 | Standard sEMG Sampling Rate | $f_s \geq$ **1 kHz** | Nyquist-compliant |
| 15 | Recommended IED | **20 mm** | SENIAM guideline |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** ML amplitude estimation is the *theoretical gold standard* for noisy sEMG analysis. In practice, **RMS** is a low-complexity proxy that is also *ML-optimal* when the underlying amplitude equals the signal standard deviation (the typical sEMG model). This duality is frequently examined in KTU papers.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — ML Estimate of Constant Amplitude

**Problem Setup:**
We observe $N$ samples $\{x(0), x(1), \ldots, x(N-1)\}$ generated by the model $x(n) = A + w(n)$, where $A$ is an unknown *deterministic* constant and $w(n)$ are i.i.d. Gaussian random variables with $w(n) \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$. The noise variance $\sigma^2$ is assumed known. Find the ML estimate of $A$.

---

**Step 1: Write the joint PDF of the observations**

Because the samples are independent, the joint PDF is the product of the marginal PDFs:

$$L(A) = f(x(0), x(1), \ldots, x(N-1); A) = \prod_{n=0}^{N-1} f(x(n); A)$$

The marginal PDF of a single sample is:

$$f(x(n); A) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2}}\,\exp\!\left(-\frac{(x(n) - A)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$$

**Explanation:** Each $x(n)$ is Gaussian with mean $A$ (the signal) and variance $\sigma^2$ (the noise). The exponent captures how "far" the observation is from the hypothesized amplitude $A$.

---

**Step 2: Form the product**

$$L(A) = \prod_{n=0}^{N-1}\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2}}\,\exp\!\left(-\frac{(x(n) - A)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$$

$$L(A) = \left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2}}\right)^{N}\,\exp\!\left(-\frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1}(x(n) - A)^2\right)$$

**Explanation:** The product of exponentials becomes an exponential of a sum — a key algebraic simplification. Likewise, the product of the constant prefactor $(1/\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2})$ raised to the $N$-th power.

---

**Step 3: Take the natural logarithm (log-likelihood)**

The logarithm is a strictly monotonically increasing function, so the value of $A$ that maximizes $\ln L(A)$ is identical to the value that maximizes $L(A)$. The log transform converts the product into a sum and the exponent into a multiplicative factor:

$$\ln L(A) = -\frac{N}{2}\ln(2\pi\sigma^2) - \frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1}(x(n) - A)^2$$

**Explanation:** $\ln\big((2\pi\sigma^2)^{-N/2}\big) = -\frac{N}{2}\ln(2\pi\sigma^2)$ and $\ln(\exp(\cdot)) = (\cdot)$. The first term is *constant* with respect to $A$ and will drop out during differentiation.

---

**Step 4: Differentiate the log-likelihood with respect to $A$**

First expand the squared term:

$$\sum_{n=0}^{N-1}(x(n) - A)^2 = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1}\big(x^2(n) - 2A\,x(n) + A^2\big)$$

Differentiate with respect to $A$, treating the data $\{x(n)\}$ as fixed constants:

$$\frac{\partial \ln L(A)}{\partial A} = -\frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1}\frac{\partial}{\partial A}\big(x^2(n) - 2A\,x(n) + A^2\big)$$

$$\frac{\partial \ln L(A)}{\partial A} = -\frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1}\big(-2x(n) + 2A\big)$$

$$\frac{\partial \ln L(A)}{\partial A} = \frac{1}{\sigma^2}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1}\big(x(n) - A\big)$$

**Explanation:** Only the $-2A\,x(n)$ and the $A^2$ terms depend on $A$. Their derivatives are $-2x(n)$ and $2A$ respectively. The minus sign in front of the fraction flips the order.

---

**Step 5: Set the derivative to zero (First-Order Necessary Condition)**

$$\frac{1}{\sigma^2}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1}\big(x(n) - A\big) = 0$$

Since $\sigma^2 > 0$, we may multiply both sides by $\sigma^2$ without changing the solution:

$$\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n) - N A = 0$$

**Explanation:** The sum of the constant $A$ over $N$ terms is $N A$, and the sum of $x(n)$ is unchanged. Pulling $A$ out of the sum is the algebraic key step.

---

**Step 6: Solve for $A$**

$$N A = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n) \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \boxed{\;\hat{A}_{\text{ML}} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n) = \bar{x}\;}$$

**Explanation:** Dividing by $N$ yields the arithmetic mean. The result is a *function of the data* — therefore a valid estimator. The ML estimate of a constant in additive white Gaussian noise is the sample mean.

---

**Step 7: Verify it is a maximum (Second-Order Condition)**

$$\frac{\partial^2 \ln L(A)}{\partial A^2} = -\frac{N}{\sigma^2} < 0$$

Because the second derivative is strictly negative, the log-likelihood is *concave* in $A$, and the critical point is unambiguously a global maximum. This is a standard property of Gaussian likelihoods.

---

## 3.2 Derivation II — General Case with Known Shape $s(n)$

**Problem Setup:**
$x(n) = A\,s(n) + w(n)$ with $s(n)$ known, $w(n) \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$, $A$ unknown.

**Step 1: Write the likelihood**

$$L(A) = (2\pi\sigma^2)^{-N/2}\exp\!\left(-\frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1}(x(n) - A s(n))^2\right)$$

**Step 2: Log-likelihood and derivative**

$$\ln L(A) = -\frac{N}{2}\ln(2\pi\sigma^2) - \frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1}\big(x(n) - A s(n)\big)^2$$

$$\frac{\partial \ln L(A)}{\partial A} = \frac{1}{\sigma^2}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} s(n)\big(x(n) - A s(n)\big) = 0$$

**Step 3: Rearrange and solve**

$$\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} s(n) x(n) - A\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} s^2(n) = 0$$

$$\boxed{\;\hat{A}_{\text{ML}} = \frac{\displaystyle\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} s(n)\, x(n)}{\displaystyle\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} s^2(n)}\;}$$

**Explanation:** The numerator is the *cross-correlation* between the observation and the known shape; the denominator is the *energy* of the shape (a normalization). This is exactly the output of a *matched filter* — a cornerstone of detection theory and a frequent KTU module 4 question.

---

## 3.3 Derivation III — Cramér–Rao Lower Bound for Constant Amplitude

**Step 1: Compute the score function**

The score is the gradient of the log-likelihood with respect to the parameter:

$$\mathcal{S}(A) = \frac{\partial \ln L(A)}{\partial A} = \frac{1}{\sigma^2}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1}(x(n) - A)$$

**Step 2: Compute the Fisher Information**

$$\mathcal{I}(A) = -\mathbb{E}\!\left[\frac{\partial^2 \ln L(A)}{\partial A^2}\right] = -\mathbb{E}\!\left[-\frac{N}{\sigma^2}\right] = \frac{N}{\sigma^2}$$

**Explanation:** The expectation is taken over the data; because the second derivative is *deterministic* (does not depend on $x(n)$), the expectation is trivial.

**Step 3: Apply the CRLB inequality**

$$\text{var}(\hat{A}) \geq \frac{1}{\mathcal{I}(A)} = \frac{\sigma^2}{N}$$

**Step 4: Show that the ML estimator is efficient**

For the ML estimator, $\text{var}(\hat{A}_{\text{ML}}) = \text{var}(\bar{x}) = \sigma^2/N$ (the variance of the sample mean for i.i.d. samples of variance $\sigma^2$). This equals the CRLB, so $\hat{A}_{\text{ML}}$ is *efficient* — it achieves the lowest possible variance among all unbiased estimators.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Properties of the ML Estimator

| Property | Statement | Status for Constant-Amp Case |
|---|---|---|
| Unbiased | $\mathbb{E}[\hat{A}] = A$ | **Yes** ($\mathbb{E}[\bar{x}] = A$) |
| Consistent | $\hat{A} \to A$ as $N \to \infty$ | **Yes** (by law of large numbers) |
| Efficient | Achieves CRLB | **Yes** |
| Asymptotically Normal | $\sqrt{N}(\hat{A} - A) \to \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$ | **Yes** |
| Invariant under re-parametrization | ML of $g(A)$ is $g(\hat{A}_{\text{ML}})$ | **Yes** |
| Sufficient statistic | $T(x) = \sum x(n)$ is sufficient | **Yes** (by factorization) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Sufficiency means that *all the information in the data about $A$ is contained in the sum* $\sum x(n)$. Any further processing that does not reduce to a function of the sum is throwing away information. The sample mean is therefore the *parsimonious* optimal estimator — a beautiful confluence of statistics, signal processing, and ergonomic minimal-latency design.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 System Block Diagram — sEMG Prosthetic Control Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Residual Limb Muscles] --> A2[Surface EMG Electrodes]
    A2 --> A3[Instrumentation Amplifier]
    A3 --> A4[Analog Bandpass Filter 20 to 500 Hz]
    A4 --> A5[Notch Filter 50 Hz]
    A5 --> A6[ADC Sampling at 1 kHz]
    A6 --> A7[DSP Sliding Window of N samples]
    A7 --> A8[Amplitude Estimator ML or RMS]
    A8 --> A9[Decision Logic Threshold or Proportional]
    A9 --> A10[DAC and Motor Driver]
    A10 --> A11[Prosthetic Actuator Hand or Elbow]
    A11 --> A12[User Feedback Visual or Vibrotactile]
    A12 --> A1
    style A8 fill:#FFD580,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style A9 fill:#A0D8FF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The yellow-highlighted "Amplitude Estimator" block is the *ML estimator* derived in Section 3. The blue-highlighted "Decision Logic" is the *prosthesis controller*. The closed loop returns to the user, capturing the *ergonomic* feedback cycle.

---

## 4.2 ML Estimation Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    B1[Input Observation Window x 0 to x N minus 1] --> B2[Initialize Likelihood L of A]
    B2 --> B3[Form Log Likelihood ln L of A]
    B3 --> B4[Differentiate with respect to A]
    B4 --> B5[Set Derivative to Zero]
    B5 --> B6[Solve Algebraic Equation for A hat]
    B6 --> B7[Verify Maximum via Second Derivative less than 0]
    B7 --> B8[Output A hat ML equals Sample Mean x bar]
    B8 --> B9[Compute Standard Error sigma over root N]
    B9 --> B10[Compare to CRLB for Efficiency Check]
    style B8 fill:#B6F0B6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style B10 fill:#FFC1C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!NOTE]
> The green block is the *ML output*; the red block is the *performance validation* step. A KTU examiner looks for the student to *both* derive the estimator and *quantify its uncertainty* — a frequent 14-mark essay structure.

---

## 4.3 Ergonomic Design Trade-Off Matrix (Mermaid Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph C1[Anatomical Constraints]
        C1A[Muscle Geometry]
        C1B[IED Selection]
        C1C[Skin Impedance]
    end

        C2A[SNR Maximization]
        C2B[Motion Artifact Suppression]
        C2C[Bandwidth Compliance]
    end
    subgraph C3[User Comfort]
        C3A[Weight of Socket]
        C3B[Sweat Tolerance]
        C3C[Donning Time]
    end

        C4A[Window Length N]
        C4B[DSP Throughput]
        C4C[Actuator Response Time]
    end
    C1 --> C2
    C2 --> C4
    C4 --> C3
    C3 --> C1
    style C1 fill:#FFE5B4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style C2 fill:#B4E5FF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style C3 fill:#D8B4FF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style C4 fill:#B4FFB4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The four ergonomic constraint blocks are *coupled*. Increasing $N$ (longer window) reduces variance ($\sigma/\sqrt{N}$) — good for signal quality — but increases latency, hurting user comfort and prosthetic responsiveness. The ergonomic optimum is the *Pareto balance* across all four quadrants.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**Q1.** Define *Ergonomics* in the context of bioelectric signal acquisition systems. List **two** quantitative ergonomic benchmarks relevant to sEMG-based prosthesis control.

**Model Answer:**
Ergonomics is the human-factors engineering discipline that designs bioelectric acquisition and prosthetic control systems to match the physiological, cognitive, and biomechanical characteristics of the human user. Two quantitative benchmarks are:
1. **Maximum acceptable controller latency:** 300 ms (clinical standard for upper-limb prosthetics).
2. **Standard sEMG electrode inter-electrode distance (IED):** 20 mm along the muscle fibre direction (SENIAM guideline).

Additional acceptable answers: usable bandwidth 20–500 Hz, skin–electrode impedance target < 5 kΩ at 50 Hz, socket mass < 300 g, power autonomy > 8 hours.

> *(3 marks: 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for two benchmarks)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Q2.** State the *standard sEMG signal model* used in amplitude estimation and identify each term.

**Model Answer:**
$$x(n) = A \cdot s(n) + w(n)$$

where:
- $x(n)$ = observed sEMG sample at discrete-time index $n$.
- $A$ = unknown deterministic amplitude parameter to be estimated (proportional to muscle activation level).
- $s(n)$ = known zero-mean, unit-variance signal *shape* (e.g., normalized MUAP template; in the simplest case $s(n) = 1$).
- $w(n) \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$ = i.i.d. additive white Gaussian noise representing electrode, electronic, and motion artifact noise.

> *(3 marks: 1 mark for equation, 2 marks for term identification)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 4, ESE]**

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Derive the Maximum Likelihood (ML) estimate of the constant amplitude $A$ in the sEMG signal model $x(n) = A + w(n)$, where $w(n) \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$ are i.i.d. and $\sigma^2$ is known. Verify that the estimate is unbiased and compute its variance. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An sEMG segment of $N = 250$ samples is recorded with sample mean $\bar{x} = 0.42$ mV and sample variance $\hat{\sigma}^2 = 0.09$ mV². Calculate the ML amplitude estimate, its standard error, and the 95% confidence interval (assume Gaussian asymptotics). Comment on the *ergonomic implication* of choosing $N = 250$ vs. $N = 100$ for prosthetic control. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(a)

**Step 1: Likelihood function**

$$L(A) = (2\pi\sigma^2)^{-N/2}\exp\!\left(-\frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1}(x(n) - A)^2\right)$$

**Step 2: Log-likelihood**

$$\ln L(A) = -\frac{N}{2}\ln(2\pi\sigma^2) - \frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1}(x(n) - A)^2$$

**Step 3: First-order condition**

$$\frac{\partial \ln L(A)}{\partial A} = \frac{1}{\sigma^2}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1}(x(n) - A) = 0$$

**Step 4: ML estimate**

$$\hat{A}_{\text{ML}} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n) = \bar{x}$$

**Step 5: Second-order condition**

$$\frac{\partial^2 \ln L(A)}{\partial A^2} = -\frac{N}{\sigma^2} < 0 \quad \text{(maximum confirmed)}$$

**Step 6: Unbiasedness**

$$\mathbb{E}[\hat{A}_{\text{ML}}] = \mathbb{E}\!\left[\frac{1}{N}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n)\right] = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1}\mathbb{E}[x(n)] = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} A = A$$

**Step 7: Variance**

$$\text{var}(\hat{A}_{\text{ML}}) = \text{var}\!\left(\frac{1}{N}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n)\right) = \frac{1}{N^2}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1}\text{var}(w(n)) = \frac{N\sigma^2}{N^2} = \frac{\sigma^2}{N}$$

The estimator achieves the CRLB, so it is **efficient**.

> *(7 marks: 2 for likelihood/log, 2 for differentiation, 1 for solution, 1 for unbiased, 1 for variance/CRLB)*

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(b)

**Step 1: ML estimate**

$$\hat{A}_{\text{ML}} = \bar{x} = 0.42 \text{ mV}$$

**Step 2: Standard error**

$$\text{SE} = \frac{\hat{\sigma}}{\sqrt{N}} = \frac{\sqrt{0.09}}{\sqrt{250}} = \frac{0.3}{15.811} \approx 0.01897 \text{ mV}$$

**Step 3: 95% Confidence interval**

For a Gaussian estimate, the 95% CI is $\hat{A} \pm 1.96 \times \text{SE}$:

$$0.42 \pm 1.96 \times 0.01897 = 0.42 \pm 0.0372 \text{ mV}$$

$$\text{CI}_{95\%} = [0.3828, \; 0.4572] \text{ mV}$$

**Step 4: Ergonomic implication**

For $N = 100$:
$$\text{SE}_{100} = \frac{0.3}{\sqrt{100}} = 0.03 \text{ mV} \quad (\text{about 58\% larger than at } N=250)$$

Larger $N$ gives better amplitude precision (smaller SE) — good for **signal quality**. However, larger $N$ also means a **longer analysis window**, which increases the **latency** of the control loop (controller can update only every $N/f_s$ seconds).

For $f_s = 1$ kHz:
- $N = 100$ ⇒ window = 100 ms (acceptable, near the 300 ms clinical limit).
- $N = 250$ ⇒ window = 250 ms (approaching the 300 ms ergonomic limit).

**Conclusion:** $N = 250$ is preferred when high-precision amplitude estimation outweighs latency concerns (e.g., force-modulation tasks). $N = 100$ is preferred for fast, discrete commands (e.g., hand open/close). The *ergonomic optimum* is task-dependent.

> *(7 marks: 2 for ML value, 1 for SE, 1 for CI, 3 for ergonomic discussion)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** With reference to the sEMG signal model $x(n) = A s(n) + w(n)$, derive the ML estimate of the amplitude parameter $A$ when the shape $s(n)$ is known. Interpret the result as a *correlator / matched filter*. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare and contrast the following prosthetic control paradigms: (i) On-Off threshold control, (ii) Proportional amplitude control, and (iii) Multi-channel pattern recognition control. Tabulate the input features, decision logic, latency profile, and suitable application class for each. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(a)

**Step 1: Likelihood function**

$$L(A) = (2\pi\sigma^2)^{-N/2}\exp\!\left(-\frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1}(x(n) - A s(n))^2\right)$$

**Step 2: Log-likelihood and differentiation**

$$\frac{\partial \ln L(A)}{\partial A} = \frac{1}{\sigma^2}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1}s(n)\big(x(n) - A s(n)\big) = 0$$

**Step 3: Solve for $A$**

$$\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} s(n) x(n) = A \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} s^2(n)$$

$$\boxed{\;\hat{A}_{\text{ML}} = \frac{\displaystyle\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} s(n) x(n)}{\displaystyle\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} s^2(n)}\;}$$

**Step 4: Correlator / Matched-filter interpretation**

The numerator $R_{sx} = \sum s(n) x(n)$ is the *cross-correlation* between the known shape and the observation — exactly the output of a *matched filter* with impulse response $h(n) = s(N-1-n)$. The denominator $E_s = \sum s^2(n)$ is the *energy* of the known shape, which normalizes the correlator output to a dimensionally correct amplitude. The structure $R_{sx}/E_s$ is the *optimum linear processor* for amplitude estimation in AWGN — a central result of detection theory.

> *(7 marks: 2 for likelihood, 2 for differentiation and solution, 3 for matched-filter interpretation)*

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(b)

| Control Paradigm | Input Feature | Decision Logic | Latency Profile | Suitable Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **(i) On-Off Threshold** | Single-channel sEMG RMS or MAV | $\hat{A} > \tau_{\text{on}}$ ⇒ ON; $\hat{A} < \tau_{\text{off}}$ ⇒ OFF | Lowest (~50–100 ms) | Simple 1-DOF terminal devices (hook, hand open/close) |
| **(ii) Proportional Amplitude** | Single-channel sEMG RMS | $u = K \hat{A}$ (linear map) | Low (~100–200 ms) | Variable-speed / variable-force 1-DOF actuators (prosthetic hand grip force) |
| **(iii) Multi-channel PR** | Multi-channel sEMG feature vector (MAV, WL, ZC, AR coefficients) | Trained classifier (LDA / SVM / MLP) | Moderate (~150–300 ms) | Multi-DOF prosthetic hands, wrists, elbows |

**Ergonomic Discussion:**
- *On-Off* is the simplest, most reliable, and lowest-latency option, but offers no graded control.
- *Proportional* is a natural extension that restores graded control, requiring only an amplitude estimator (the ML estimate / RMS).
- *Pattern recognition* provides the richest control (multiple gestures → multiple actuator commands) but at the cost of higher computational latency, larger training burden, and reduced robustness to electrode shift (a major ergonomic concern).

> *(7 marks: 2 marks per paradigm × 3 = 6 marks, plus 1 mark for synthesis / ergonomic summary)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks (Module 4):**
> 1. **Confusing $A$ with the instantaneous sEMG sample value.** $A$ is a *parameter* (the unknown muscle activation level), not a sample. Saying "the ML estimate is the maximum value of $x(n)$" is incorrect and costs 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the $\sigma^2$ in the denominator** when writing the Gaussian PDF. This is a frequent 1-mark deduction in Part A.
> 3. **Not verifying the second-order condition** in ML derivations. KTU examiners explicitly check whether the student has *proved* the estimate is a maximum (not a minimum or saddle).
> 4. **Omitting the "unbiased" verification.** The full 7-mark derivation in Q.A(a) explicitly requires showing $\mathbb{E}[\hat{A}] = A$ for full credit.
> 5. **Mixing up $A$ and $s(n)$** in the general model. The shape $s(n)$ is *known* (e.g., a normalized MUAP template); the amplitude $A$ is the *unknown*. Mis-stating this costs 2 marks in 14-mark answers.
> 6. **Failing to comment on the latency vs. precision trade-off** in ergonomic questions. The examiner expects a quantitative comparison (e.g., SE scales as $1/\sqrt{N}$ while latency scales as $N$).
> 7. **Not citing the CRLB result.** The statement "the ML estimator is *efficient*" carries weight only if accompanied by the CRLB equality $\text{var}(\hat{A}) = \sigma^2/N$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- [ ] **Ergonomics** = matching bioelectric systems to the human user's anatomical, cognitive, and biomechanical constraints. Key benchmarks: latency < **300 ms**, IED = **20 mm**, bandwidth = **20–500 Hz**, $f_s \geq$ **1 kHz**.
- [ ] **Prosthesis control** maps sEMG features to actuator commands. Paradigms: On-Off, Proportional, Pattern Recognition, Regression, Adaptive.
- [ ] **sEMG signal model (general):** $x(n) = A s(n) + w(n)$, with $w(n) \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$ i.i.d. The simplest case is $s(n) = 1$, giving $x(n) = A + w(n)$.
- [ ] **ML estimate of constant $A$:** $\hat{A}_{\text{ML}} = \bar{x} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n)$.
- [ ] **ML estimate with known shape $s(n)$:** $\hat{A}_{\text{ML}} = \frac{\sum s(n) x(n)}{\sum s^2(n)}$ — a *correlator / matched-filter* output.
- [ ] **ML estimator properties:** unbiased, consistent, **efficient** (achieves CRLB), sufficient, asymptotically normal.
- [ ] **Cramér–Rao Lower Bound:** $\text{var}(\hat{A}) \geq \sigma^2 / N$, with equality for the ML estimator.
- [ ] **Standard error of the amplitude estimate:** $\text{SE} = \sigma/\sqrt{N}$, decreasing as $1/\sqrt{N}$.
- [ ] **Practical proxy for amplitude:** RMS and MAV are the two most common sEMG amplitude features used in real-time prosthetic controllers.
- [ ] **Trade-off mantra:** Larger $N$ → better precision (smaller SE) but higher latency (worse ergonomics). Optimal $N$ balances these for the specific task.
- [ ] **Confidence interval formula:** $\hat{A} \pm 1.96 \cdot \text{SE}$ (95% CI, Gaussian asymptotics).
- [ ] **Match the derivation to the marks:** Likelihood → Log-likelihood → Differentiate → Set to zero → Solve → Verify maximum → Check unbiasedness → Compute variance → State CRLB. This 9-step template covers most 7-mark ML questions in KTU ESE papers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Spectral Analysis of Surface EMG (sEMG)

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):**
Spectral analysis of surface EMG refers to the decomposition of the surface electromyographic signal into its frequency components to characterize the underlying motor unit action potential (MUAP) trains. It is typically represented as a **Power Spectral Density (PSD)** function $P(f)$, from which parameters like **Mean Power Frequency (MPF)** and **Median Frequency (MF)** are extracted to assess muscle physiology, fatigue, and pathology.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Surface EMG is a *spatially filtered* and *temporally summed* representation of all active motor units beneath the electrodes. The spectrum therefore encodes information about motor unit recruitment, firing rates, and the muscle fiber conduction velocity (MFCV).

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Imagine you are standing in a stadium and 50,000 fans are clapping. You cannot hear any single clap. Instead, you hear a continuous "roar." The frequency content of this roar tells you whether the claps are fast, slow, or irregular. Similarly, surface EMG is a continuous "roar" of thousands of motor unit firings. Spectral analysis is the mathematical tool that translates this roar into a graph showing *which* frequencies dominate — and this graph is a fingerprint of muscle health.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Bandwidth of sEMG:** The useful energy of sEMG lies in the range of **5 Hz to 500 Hz**, with the dominant power concentrated between **20 Hz and 200 Hz**. The upper cutoff depends on inter-electrode distance and the sharpness of the intramuscular action potentials.

---

## 1.2 Muscle Fiber Conduction Velocity (MFCV) Estimation

**Formal Definition:**
Muscle Fiber Conduction Velocity (MFCV) is the speed at which an action potential propagates along a single muscle fiber, measured in **meters per second (m/s)**. In sEMG, it is estimated indirectly using two or more electrodes placed along the muscle fiber direction and computing the time delay between the recorded signals.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physiological Range of MFCV:** Normal adult skeletal muscle exhibits MFCV in the range of **3 m/s to 6 m/s** (typical value ~4 m/s for biceps brachii at body temperature). MFCV decreases during **fatigue** and is altered in **myopathies** and **neuropathies**.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Picture a group of runners racing along a track. If you photograph them at two points 50 meters apart, the time delay between the two snapshots reveals their speed. For muscle fibers, the two "photographs" are the bipolar electrode recordings, and the "speed" is the conduction velocity. A slower runner (fatigued fiber) produces a larger time gap between the two recordings.

---

## 1.3 Intramuscular EMG Signal Decomposition

**Formal Definition:**
Intramuscular EMG decomposition is the process of resolving a complex needle EMG recording (which is a superposition of multiple motor unit action potential trains) into its constituent **individual MUAP trains**, each characterized by its waveform template, firing instants, and assignment to a specific motor unit.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Imagine a cocktail party where 5 people are speaking simultaneously into a single microphone. The recorded audio is a jumbled mess. Intramuscular EMG decomposition is the computational equivalent of an "audio separator" that isolates each speaker's voice, identifies when they spoke, and labels them as Person 1, Person 2, etc. In clinical neurophysiology, the "speakers" are the **anterior horn cells** and the "voices" are their **motor unit action potentials (MUAPs)**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Clinical Relevance:** Decomposition is the gold-standard computational method for diagnosing disorders such as **amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS)**, **myasthenia gravis**, and **radiculopathies**, where MUAP morphology and firing patterns are diagnostically critical.

---

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Power Spectral Density (PSD) of sEMG — Theoretical Basis

The sEMG signal $x(t)$ is modeled as a **stochastic process** — a zero-mean, wide-sense stationary (locally) random signal. Its second-order statistics are fully characterized by the autocorrelation function $R_x(\tau)$ and its Fourier transform, the PSD $P_x(f)$.

**Why is sEMG stochastic?**
- Motor unit firing is a quasi-random point process (Poisson-like with refractoriness).
- The number of active motor units varies stochastically with voluntary force.
- Superposition of asynchronous MUAPs produces an interference pattern.

**Estimation Methods for PSD:**

1. **Welch's Method (Non-parametric, Periodogram-based):**
   - Segment the signal into overlapping windows of length $L$.
   - Apply a window function $w(n)$ (e.g., Hann).
   - Compute the squared magnitude of the FFT for each segment.
   - Average across all $K$ segments.
   
2. **Autoregressive (AR) Parametric Model:**
   - Model $x(n)$ as: $x(n) = -\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k \, x(n-k) + e(n)$, where $e(n)$ is white noise.
   - PSD: $P_x(f) = \dfrac{\sigma_e^2}{\vert 1 + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k e^{-j2\pi f k} \vert^2}$
   - Better spectral resolution for short data segments.

3. **Maximum Entropy Method (MEM):**
   - Equivalent to AR modeling; yields smooth, high-resolution spectra.

**Spectral Parameters Used in KTU Assessments:**

- **Mean Power Frequency (MPF):**  
  $$\text{MPF} = \frac{\int_0^{f_s/2} f \cdot P(f) \, df}{\int_0^{f_s/2} P(f) \, df}$$

- **Median Frequency (MF):** The frequency $f_{\text{med}}$ such that:  
  $$\int_0^{f_{\text{med}}} P(f) \, df = \frac{1}{2} \int_0^{f_s/2} P(f) \, df$$

- **Spectral Entropy:**  
  $$H = -\sum_{i=1}^{N} p_i \log_2 p_i, \quad \text{where } p_i = \frac{P(f_i)}{\sum_{i} P(f_i)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why MPF and MF are critical:** During sustained isometric contraction, the sEMG spectrum **compresses toward lower frequencies** (spectral compression). This is the classical signature of **muscle fatigue** and is attributed to a reduction in MFCV. MPF and MF decrease monotonically with fatigue.

---

## 2.2 Muscle Fiber Conduction Velocity (MFCV) — Estimation Methods

### Method A: Cross-Correlation Method (Time Domain)

Two sEMG signals $x_1(t)$ and $x_2(t)$ are recorded from two electrodes separated by a known distance $d$ along the muscle fiber direction. The time delay $\tau$ between them is estimated as the lag corresponding to the peak of the cross-correlation function $R_{12}(\tau)$.

$$R_{12}(\tau) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x_1(t) \, x_2(t+\tau) \, dt$$

$$\hat{\tau} = \arg\max_{\tau} \, R_{12}(\tau)$$

$$\hat{v} = \frac{d}{\hat{\tau}}$$

### Method B: Phase / Spectral Difference Method (Frequency Domain)

The cross-spectrum $S_{12}(f)$ is computed from the two channels. The **phase difference** at frequency $f$ is:

$$\Delta \phi(f) = \angle S_{12}(f)$$

For a propagating wave with velocity $v$ across electrode distance $d$, the phase delay corresponds to a time delay $\tau = d/v$, so:

$$\Delta \phi(f) = 2\pi f \cdot \frac{d}{v}$$

Rearranging:

$$v = \frac{2\pi f \, d}{\Delta \phi(f)}$$

In practice, a linear fit of $\Delta \phi(f)$ versus $f$ yields a robust estimate of $v$:

$$\hat{v} = \frac{2\pi \, d}{\text{slope of } \Delta\phi(f) \text{ vs } f}$$

### Method C: Maximum Likelihood (ML) Estimator

For Gaussian-distributed sEMG, the ML estimate of $\tau$ minimizes the mean squared error between the two signals. It is essentially equivalent to a weighted cross-correlation and is asymptotically **efficient** (achieves Cramér-Rao lower bound).

### Method D: Spectral Peak Tracking

When MFCV decreases (e.g., fatigue), the **center frequency** of the sEMG PSD shifts. The relationship is:

$$f_c \approx \frac{v}{2 d_{MUAP}}$$

where $d_{MUAP}$ is the characteristic width of a single MUAP. By tracking $f_c$ over time, $v$ can be continuously monitored.

---

## 2.3 Intramuscular EMG Decomposition — Algorithmic Frameworks

**The Decomposition Problem Statement:**
Given a needle EMG signal $y(n) = \sum_{m=1}^{M} \sum_{k} h_m(n - t_{m,k}) + \eta(n)$, estimate the number of motor units $M$, their MUAP templates $h_m$, and their firing times $t_{m,k}$.

### Method 1: Template Matching (Classical)
- Detect all peaks above a threshold.
- Cluster peaks by waveform shape using feature vectors (e.g., area, duration, peak-to-peak amplitude, number of turns).
- Average clusters to form templates.
- Assign each detected MUAP to the nearest template (minimum Euclidean distance).

### Method 2: Wavelet-Based Decomposition
- Apply **Discrete Wavelet Transform (DWT)** with mother wavelet (e.g., Daubechies-4).
- Detect MUAPs at scales corresponding to the MUAP bandwidth.
- Use **wavelet packet decomposition** for finer frequency localization.
- Classify using clustering in the wavelet coefficient space.

### Method 3: Independent Component Analysis (ICA) / Blind Source Separation
- Model EMG as linear mixture: $\mathbf{x}(n) = \mathbf{A} \mathbf{s}(n)$, where $\mathbf{s}(n)$ contains the MUAP trains (assumed statistically independent).
- Apply **FastICA** or **Infomax** to estimate the unmixing matrix $\mathbf{W} \approx \mathbf{A}^{-1}$.
- Recover: $\hat{\mathbf{s}}(n) = \mathbf{W} \mathbf{x}(n)$.

### Method 4: Artificial Neural Network (ANN) Classifiers
- Train a multi-layer perceptron (MLP) or radial basis function (RBF) network to classify MUAPs into motor unit classes.
- Use features like waveform area, durations, peak-to-peak, and turn count as input.

### Method 5: Expert System / Hybrid Methods (EMG-LODEC, EMGLAB)
- Combine **template matching** with **firing-time statistics** (e.g., interdischarge interval histogram).
- Use **k-means clustering** with adaptive thresholding.
- Commercial tools: *EMGLAB* (Matlab-based), *DQEMG*.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description / Use | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_x(f) = \mathcal{F}\{R_x(\tau)\}$ | PSD via Wiener-Khinchin theorem | $P_x(f) \ge 0$ |
| 2 | $P_x(f) = \frac{\sigma_e^2}{\vert 1 + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k e^{-j2\pi fk} \vert^2}$ | AR model PSD | Parametric |
| 3 | $\text{MPF} = \frac{\int f \cdot P(f)\, df}{\int P(f)\, df}$ | Mean Power Frequency | Hz |
| 4 | $\int_0^{f_{\text{med}}} P(f)\, df = \frac{1}{2} \int_0^{\infty} P(f)\, df$ | Median Frequency definition | Hz |
| 5 | $v = d / \tau$ | Conduction velocity from time delay | m/s |
| 6 | $\Delta\phi(f) = 2\pi f \cdot d/v$ | Phase-method CV relation | Radians |
| 7 | $\hat{v} = 2\pi d / \text{slope}[\Delta\phi(f) \text{ vs } f]$ | Linear-phase CV estimator | m/s |
| 8 | $f_c \approx v / (2 d_{MUAP})$ | Spectral-center vs MFCV link | Hz |
| 9 | $\hat{\tau} = \arg\max_\tau \, R_{12}(\tau)$ | ML / cross-correlation delay | seconds |
| 10 | $H = -\sum p_i \log_2 p_i$ | Spectral entropy | bits |
| 11 | $\text{CRLB}(\hat{v}) = \frac{c \cdot v^3}{f_c^3 \cdot N \cdot \text{SNR}^2}$ | Cramér-Rao lower bound for CV | Cramér-Rao inequality |
| 12 | $y(n) = \sum_{m,k} h_m(n - t_{m,k}) + \eta(n)$ | Intramuscular EMG superposition model | Discrete-time |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** MFCV monitoring is widely used in **occupational ergonomics** (preventing work-related musculoskeletal disorders), **sports science** (training load optimization), **rehabilitation engineering** (biofeedback for stroke patients), and **prosthetic control** (MFCV-based mode switching).

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: MFCV from the Cross-Spectrum Phase Slope

**Starting Premise:** Consider a propagating depolarization wave traveling along a muscle fiber with constant velocity $v$ between two surface electrodes separated by distance $d$.

**Step 1:** The time delay $\tau$ between the two electrode recordings is:
$$\tau = \frac{d}{v}$$

**Step 2:** In the frequency domain, a time delay corresponds to a linear phase shift. The Fourier transform of a delayed signal satisfies:
$$x_2(t) = x_1(t - \tau) \;\Longleftrightarrow\; X_2(f) = X_1(f) e^{-j 2\pi f \tau}$$

**Step 3:** The cross-spectrum $S_{12}(f)$ is:
$$S_{12}(f) = X_1^*(f) X_2(f) = X_1^*(f) X_1(f) e^{-j 2\pi f \tau} = \vert X_1(f) \vert^2 e^{-j 2\pi f \tau}$$

**Step 4:** The phase of the cross-spectrum is therefore:
$$\Delta \phi(f) = \angle S_{12}(f) = -2\pi f \tau = -2\pi f \cdot \frac{d}{v}$$

**Step 5:** Substituting $\tau = d/v$:
$$\Delta \phi(f) = -\frac{2\pi f d}{v}$$

**Step 6:** Solving for $v$ at a single frequency $f$:
$$v = -\frac{2\pi f d}{\Delta \phi(f)}$$

**Step 7:** To improve robustness, we perform a **linear regression** of $\Delta \phi(f)$ versus $f$ over a useful frequency band (e.g., 20–250 Hz). The slope of this line is:
$$\text{slope} = \frac{\Delta \phi}{\Delta f} = -\frac{2\pi d}{v}$$

**Step 8:** Final MFCV estimator (note the negative sign — slope is negative for forward-propagating waves):
$$\boxed{\hat{v} = -\frac{2\pi d}{\text{slope}}}$$

**Numerical Sanity Check:** If $d = 0.025$ m (2.5 cm inter-electrode spacing) and the measured slope is $-39.27$ rad/Hz (a typical value at 4 m/s), then:
$$\hat{v} = -\frac{2\pi \times 0.025}{-39.27} = \frac{0.1571}{39.27} \approx 0.00400 \text{ km/ms} = 4.00 \text{ m/s} \;\; \checkmark$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Mean Power Frequency from PSD Moments

**Starting Premise:** The PSD $P(f)$ of sEMG is a real, even, non-negative function. We define the **zeroth spectral moment** $M_0$ and the **first spectral moment** $M_1$.

**Step 1:** Define the zeroth moment (total signal power):
$$M_0 = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} P(f) \, df = \int_0^{f_s/2} P(f) \, df \quad \text{(for real signal, halved factor of 2)}$$

**Step 2:** Define the first moment (weighted by frequency):
$$M_1 = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f \cdot P(f) \, df = \int_0^{f_s/2} f \cdot P(f) \, df$$

**Step 3:** The Mean Power Frequency is the ratio:
$$\text{MPF} = \frac{M_1}{M_0}$$

**Step 4:** Substituting the integral forms:
$$\text{MPF} = \frac{\int_0^{f_s/2} f \cdot P(f) \, df}{\int_0^{f_s/2} P(f) \, df}$$

**Step 5:** Express in discrete form (with $N$ frequency bins, $f_k = k \cdot \Delta f$):
$$\widehat{\text{MPF}} = \frac{\sum_{k=1}^{N/2} f_k \cdot P(f_k)}{\sum_{k=1}^{N/2} P(f_k)}$$

**Step 6:** When MFCV decreases (fatigue), the PSD "shifts left" — more power accumulates at lower frequencies. Therefore $M_1$ decreases faster than $M_0$, leading to a **decrease in MPF** — this is the **spectral compression** phenomenon.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Cramér-Rao Lower Bound for MFCV Estimation

**Starting Premise:** For a Gaussian WSS process with PSD $P(f)$, the variance of any unbiased delay estimator $\hat{\tau}$ is bounded by:

$$\text{var}(\hat{\tau}) \ge \left[ \int_{-f_s/2}^{f_s/2} \frac{(2\pi f)^2 \, P(f)^2}{1 - \vert \gamma(f) \vert^2} \, df \right]^{-1}$$

where $\gamma(f) = S_{12}(f)/\sqrt{S_{11}(f) S_{22}(f)}$ is the coherence function.

**Step 1:** For high SNR and unit coherence ($\vert \gamma(f) \vert \approx 1$ in the signal band), the bound simplifies to:
$$\text{var}(\hat{\tau}) \ge \frac{1}{(2\pi)^2 \int_{-f_s/2}^{f_s/2} f^2 \, P(f)^2 \, df}$$

**Step 2:** Define the **effective bandwidth** $B_{\text{eff}}$:
$$B_{\text{eff}}^2 = \frac{\int f^2 P(f)^2 df}{\int P(f)^2 df}$$

**Step 3:** The bound becomes:
$$\text{var}(\hat{\tau}) \ge \frac{1}{(2\pi)^2 \, T \cdot \text{SNR} \cdot B_{\text{eff}}^2}$$

**Step 4:** Since $v = d/\tau$, using the delta method for variance propagation:
$$\text{var}(\hat{v}) = \left( \frac{\partial v}{\partial \tau} \right)^2 \text{var}(\hat{\tau}) = \left( \frac{d}{\tau^2} \right)^2 \text{var}(\hat{\tau}) = \left( \frac{v^2}{d} \right)^2 \text{var}(\hat{\tau})$$

**Step 5:** Substituting:
$$\boxed{\text{var}(\hat{v}) \ge \frac{v^4}{(2\pi)^2 \, d^2 \, T \cdot \text{SNR} \cdot B_{\text{eff}}^2}}$$

**Key Insight:** To improve CV estimation accuracy, we need:
- **Larger electrode separation $d$** (subject to volume-conductor constraints)
- **Longer observation window $T$**
- **Higher SNR** (good electrode-skin contact, low motion artifact)
- **Broader effective bandwidth $B_{\text{eff}}$**

---

## 3.4 Worked Example: Cross-Correlation MFCV Estimation

**Given:**
- Two sEMG channels from the biceps brachii.
- Inter-electrode distance: $d = 30$ mm $= 0.030$ m.
- Sampling rate: $f_s = 2000$ Hz.
- Window length: $N = 4096$ samples (2.048 s).
- Estimated delay from cross-correlation peak: $\hat{\tau} = 7.5$ ms.

**Step 1:** Compute MFCV:
$$\hat{v} = \frac{d}{\hat{\tau}} = \frac{0.030}{0.0075} = 4.0 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2:** Convert delay in samples (for reference):
$$\hat{\tau}_{\text{samples}} = \hat{\tau} \times f_s = 0.0075 \times 2000 = 15 \text{ samples}$$

**Step 3:** Check physiological plausibility: 4.0 m/s is within the normal range (3–6 m/s) for biceps. ✓

**Step 4:** Compute the spectral shift expected at 4 m/s with $d_{\text{MUAP}} \approx 0.001$ m:
$$f_c \approx \frac{v}{2 d_{\text{MUAP}}} = \frac{4.0}{2 \times 0.001} = 2000 \text{ Hz}$$

This is higher than the typical sEMG upper bound (~500 Hz) because $d_{\text{MUAP}}$ in the above is a simplified model. In practice, $d_{\text{MUAP}}$ is interpreted as the effective spatial spread of the depolarization zone, which yields realistic $f_c$ values in the 60–120 Hz range.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Algorithm Selection Guide

| Scenario | Recommended PSD Method | Recommended MFCV Method | Decomposition Method |
|---|---|---|---|
| Short data window, high SNR | AR / MEM | Maximum Likelihood | ANN-based |
| Long data, moderate SNR | Welch (averaged periodogram) | Cross-correlation | Template matching |
| Multi-channel array | Multi-taper | Phase-slope (multi-channel) | ICA / BSS |
| Real-time clinical use | Recursive AR | Single-channel phase | Expert system (EMGLAB) |
| Fatigue monitoring | Welch + MPF tracking | Phase-slope with Kalman filter | Not required (global signal) |

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Define Mean Power Frequency (MPF) of an sEMG signal. Explain its significance in assessing muscle fatigue.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1.5 marks):** The Mean Power Frequency is the average frequency of the surface EMG power spectrum, computed as a power-weighted mean:  
  $$\text{MPF} = \frac{\int_0^{f_s/2} f \cdot P(f) \, df}{\int_0^{f_s/2} P(f) \, df}$$
- **Significance (1.5 marks):** During sustained muscle contraction, MPF decreases monotonically due to **spectral compression** caused by reduced muscle fiber conduction velocity (MFCV) and synchronization of motor unit firing. The **slope of MPF vs. time** is a well-established, non-invasive **fatigue index** in ergonomics, sports science, and rehabilitation.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**What is Muscle Fiber Conduction Velocity (MFCV)? List any two methods used to estimate it from surface EMG.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** MFCV is the speed at which the depolarization wave propagates along a skeletal muscle fiber, expressed in **m/s**, with a normal physiological range of **3–6 m/s**.
- **Method 1 (1 mark):** **Cross-correlation method** — the time delay $\hat{\tau}$ is found at the peak of the cross-correlation function $R_{12}(\tau)$ between two sEMG channels separated by distance $d$, giving $v = d/\hat{\tau}$.
- **Method 2 (1 mark):** **Phase-slope method** — the slope of the unwrapped cross-spectral phase $\Delta\phi(f)$ vs. $f$ is computed; the velocity is $v = -2\pi d / \text{slope}$.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the spectral analysis of surface EMG using the **Welch periodogram method**. Derive the expressions for **Mean Power Frequency** and **Median Frequency** from the PSD. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **cross-correlation method** for estimating muscle fiber conduction velocity. A bipolar sEMG signal is recorded with an inter-electrode distance of **25 mm**, and the estimated time delay is **6.25 ms**. Compute the MFCV and comment on whether the result is within the physiological range. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to Q-A(a)

**Block Diagram (2 marks):**
Signal → Segmentation (overlapping windows) → Windowing (Hann) → FFT per segment → Magnitude squared → Ensemble averaging → $P_x(f)$ → Feature extraction (MPF, MF).

**Welch Method Steps (3 marks):**
1. Divide $x(n)$ of length $N$ into $K$ overlapping segments of length $L$: $x_k(n) = x(n + kD)$ for $k = 0, 1, \dots, K-1$, with hop $D$.
2. Apply a window $w(n)$ (e.g., Hann) to each segment.
3. Compute the periodogram:  
   $$\hat{P}_k(f) = \frac{1}{L \cdot U} \left\vert \sum_{n=0}^{L-1} w(n) \, x_k(n) \, e^{-j 2\pi f n} \right\vert^2$$
   where $U = \frac{1}{L} \sum w^2(n)$ is the window energy normalization.
4. Average across segments:  
   $$\hat{P}_x(f) = \frac{1}{K} \sum_{k=0}^{K-1} \hat{P}_k(f)$$

**Derivation of MPF and MF (2 marks):**
- MPF derivation as in Section 3.2: $M_0 = \int P(f) df$, $M_1 = \int f P(f) df$, $\text{MPF} = M_1 / M_0$.
- MF: the unique $f_{\text{med}}$ satisfying the half-power condition.

---

#### Solution to Q-A(b)

**Cross-Correlation Method (3 marks):**
1. Two sEMG signals $x_1(t)$, $x_2(t)$ recorded with inter-electrode distance $d$.
2. Compute $R_{12}(\tau) = \int x_1(t) x_2(t + \tau) \, dt$.
3. Locate $\hat{\tau} = \arg\max_\tau R_{12}(\tau)$.
4. Estimate: $\hat{v} = d / \hat{\tau}$.

**Computation (3 marks):**
$$d = 25 \text{ mm} = 0.025 \text{ m}$$
$$\hat{\tau} = 6.25 \text{ ms} = 6.25 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s}$$
$$\hat{v} = \frac{0.025}{6.25 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{0.025}{0.00625} = 4.0 \text{ m/s}$$

**Comment (1 mark):** The result **4.0 m/s** lies comfortably within the normal physiological range of **3–6 m/s** for healthy adult skeletal muscle, indicating no obvious neuromuscular pathology.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the **phase-slope method** for MFCV estimation. Derive the relation between conduction velocity, electrode spacing, and the slope of the cross-spectral phase. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the algorithmic steps for **intramuscular EMG signal decomposition** using template matching. What are the limitations of this approach, and how can they be overcome? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to Q-B(a)

**Phase-Slope Method (3 marks):**
- Two sEMG signals $x_1(t)$ and $x_2(t)$ from electrodes separated by $d$.
- Compute the cross-spectrum $S_{12}(f) = X_1^*(f) X_2(f)$.
- Unwrap the phase $\Delta \phi(f) = \angle S_{12}(f)$.
- Perform a linear regression of $\Delta \phi(f)$ vs. $f$ over the signal bandwidth.

**Derivation (3 marks):**
From the delayed-signal model $x_2(t) = x_1(t - d/v)$, the cross-spectrum is:
$$S_{12}(f) = \vert X_1(f) \vert^2 e^{-j 2\pi f d / v}$$

The phase is:
$$\Delta \phi(f) = -\frac{2\pi f d}{v}$$

The slope of $\Delta \phi$ vs. $f$ is:
$$\text{slope} = -\frac{2\pi d}{v}$$

Hence:
$$\boxed{\hat{v} = -\frac{2\pi d}{\text{slope}}}$$

**Advantages (1 mark):** Robust to noise, uses the entire signal bandwidth, and provides a goodness-of-fit measure (coherence).

---

#### Solution to Q-B(b)

**Template Matching Steps (3 marks):**
1. **Preprocessing:** Bandpass filter (20 Hz – 5 kHz), 50/60 Hz notch.
2. **Peak Detection:** Identify all candidate MUAPs using a sliding-window threshold.
3. **Feature Extraction:** For each peak, compute area, peak-to-peak amplitude, duration, number of turns/spikes.
4. **Initial Clustering:** Apply k-means or hierarchical clustering to form preliminary motor-unit groups.
5. **Template Formation:** Average all MUAPs in each cluster to form a representative template.
6. **Template Matching:** Assign each detected MUAP to the nearest template (minimum Euclidean distance).
7. **Validation:** Check the inter-spike interval (ISI) histogram for refractory period (~1–5 ms minimum).

**Limitations (2 marks):**
- **Superimposition of MUAPs** from multiple motor units is misclassified.
- **MUAP shape variability** (e.g., due to electrode jitter) causes unstable templates.
- **Template drift** over long recordings reduces accuracy.
- Difficulty in handling **low-amplitude or distant** motor units.

**Improvements (2 marks):**
- **Iterative refinement:** re-estimate templates after assignment and re-classify.
- **Artificial Neural Networks / Deep Learning** (e.g., CNNs) for non-linear feature classification.
- **Wavelet-based features** for time-frequency discrimination.
- **Independent Component Analysis (ICA)** for blind source separation when multi-channel data is available.
- **Bayesian frameworks** with probabilistic templates to handle variability.

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency (–1 mark):** Mixing ms with seconds, mm with meters when computing MFCV. Always convert to SI: $d$ in meters, $\tau$ in seconds, $v$ in m/s.
> 2. **Missing Window Normalization (–1 mark):** In Welch's method, students often write the periodogram without the $\frac{1}{L \cdot U}$ normalization factor, losing a mark.
> 3. **Confusing MPF and MF (–1 mark):** MPF is a weighted mean; MF is the **half-power** point. They are *not* equal for skewed spectra.
> 4. **Forgetting the Negative Sign in Phase-Slope (–1 mark):** $\text{slope} = -2\pi d / v$ — students often drop the sign, leading to a negative MFCV.
> 5. **No Coherence Check (–2 marks in clinical questions):** Phase-slope estimates are only valid when **coherence** $\vert \gamma(f) \vert^2 > 0.7$. Always verify coherence before trusting the CV estimate.
> 6. **In Decomposition: Ignoring Refractory Period (–1 mark):** A valid motor unit must have a minimum inter-discharge interval of ~1 ms. ISI histograms must be checked.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Spectral Analysis, MFCV, and EMG Decomposition:**

- ⭐ sEMG spectrum lies in **5–500 Hz**; dominant power in **20–200 Hz**.
- ⭐ PSD is estimated by **Welch (non-parametric)** or **AR (parametric)** methods.
- ⭐ **MPF** = spectral centroid; **MF** = half-power point; both **decrease with fatigue**.
- ⭐ MFCV normal range = **3–6 m/s**; decreases with fatigue and pathology.
- ⭐ Two main MFCV methods: **Cross-correlation (time domain)** and **Phase-slope (frequency domain)**.
- ⭐ Phase-slope relation: $v = -2\pi d / \text{slope}[\Delta \phi(f) \text{ vs } f]$.
- ⭐ Cross-correlation: $v = d / \hat{\tau}$, where $\hat{\tau} = \arg\max R_{12}(\tau)$.
- ⭐ **Spectral compression** = shift of PSD toward lower frequencies = fatigue signature.
- ⭐ CRLB for MFCV decreases with longer $T$, larger $d$, higher SNR, and broader $B_{\text{eff}}$.
- ⭐ Intramuscular EMG = superposition of MUAP trains from many motor units.
- ⭐ Decomposition goal: recover individual MUAP templates and firing times.
- ⭐ Methods: Template matching, Wavelet, ICA, ANN, Expert Systems (EMGLAB, LODEC).
- ⭐ Template matching workflow: Detect → Feature → Cluster → Template → Assign → Validate ISI.
- ⭐ **Superimposition** is the hardest problem in decomposition; ICA and deep learning offer modern solutions.
- ⭐ **Coherence check** ($\vert \gamma(f) \vert^2 > 0.7$) is mandatory before trusting MFCV estimates.
- ⭐ MUAP = Motor Unit Action Potential; motor unit = one α-motor neuron + all fibers it innervates.
